Prostaglandin E1 (alprostadil) is started at 5 to 10 nanograms/kg/min and may be reduced to 2 to 5 nanograms/kg/min once the ductus reopens. The infant must be managed in a setting prepared for intubation as apnoea occurs in approximately 12% of neonates receiving prostaglandin infusion.

subtopic_detail: Acute rheumatic fever - Jones criteria and secondary prophylaxis
stem:
A 9-year-old Aboriginal boy from regional Queensland presents with a 1-week history of fever and painful joints. His mother reports that his knees were swollen and painful initially, then his ankles became affected as his knees improved. He had a sore throat 3 weeks ago that was not treated with antibiotics. He has no history of previous rheumatic fever. On examination his temperature is 38.6 degrees C.

His right ankle is swollen, warm, and tender. Cardiac auscultation reveals a grade 3 out of 6 pansystolic murmur at the apex radiating to the axilla. There is no rash, no subcutaneous nodules, and no involuntary movements.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110, RR 22, Temp 38.6 degrees C, BP 100/65 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Right ankle swollen and tender, pansystolic murmur at apex radiating to axilla, no chorea, no erythema marginatum, no subcutaneous nodules
data_table.Investigations: ASO titre 480 IU/mL (elevated), CRP 85 mg/L, ESR 65 mm/hr, WCC 13.2 x10^9/L. Echocardiogram: mild mitral regurgitation with mitral valve thickening. Throat swab: negative for group A streptococcus

lead_in: What is the most important management step after confirming the diagnosis?

options.text: Prescribe oral amoxicillin for 10 days to treat the pharyngitis
options.rationale: While eradication of group A streptococcal carriage with antibiotics is part of initial management, a single course of oral amoxicillin addresses only the acute infection. The critical step is initiating secondary prophylaxis with IM benzathine penicillin G to prevent recurrent streptococcal infections and further rheumatic heart disease progression. (Source: RHD Australia Guideline)

options.text: Commence IM benzathine penicillin G for secondary prophylaxis
options.rationale: This child meets the Australian modified Jones criteria for acute rheumatic fever in a high-risk population (2 major criteria: carditis and migratory polyarthritis, plus evidence of preceding streptococcal infection). Secondary prophylaxis with IM benzathine penicillin G (450 mg for less than 20 kg or 900 mg for 20 kg or more) every 28 days is the most critical management step to prevent recurrence. (Source: RHD Australia Guideline)

options.text: Commence high-dose aspirin as primary treatment for this episode
options.rationale: High-dose aspirin or naproxen can be used for symptomatic management of joint inflammation in acute rheumatic fever, but this does not address the prevention of recurrent episodes. Anti-inflammatory treatment alone without secondary prophylaxis leaves the child at high risk of recurrent rheumatic fever and progressive valvular damage. (Source: eTG - Acute Rheumatic Fever)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone for the carditis and joint symptoms
options.rationale: Oral prednisolone may be considered for moderate to severe carditis causing heart failure, but this child has only mild mitral regurgitation without haemodynamic compromise. Corticosteroids do not prevent long-term valvular damage and are not a substitute for secondary prophylaxis, which is the priority intervention. (Source: RHD Australia Guideline)

options.text: Observe and recheck the ASO titre in 4 weeks before treatment
options.rationale: Delaying treatment to await repeat serology is inappropriate when the clinical presentation already satisfies the diagnostic criteria for acute rheumatic fever. The ASO titre is already elevated, and combined with the clinical findings, the diagnosis is established. Delay risks ongoing cardiac damage and recurrent streptococcal exposure. (Source: eTG - Acute Rheumatic Fever)

explanation.summary: This child meets the Australian modified Jones criteria for acute rheumatic fever with two major manifestations (carditis with echocardiographic mitral regurgitation, and migratory polyarthritis) plus evidence of preceding streptococcal infection (elevated ASO titre). In Australian Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander populations, modified lower-threshold criteria apply. The most critical management step is initiating lifelong secondary prophylaxis with IM benzathine penicillin G every 28 days to prevent recurrent episodes that cause progressive rheumatic heart disease.
key_points: Australian modified Jones criteria have a lower threshold for high-risk populations including Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children
Secondary prophylaxis with IM benzathine penicillin G every 28 days is the key intervention
Duration of prophylaxis depends on presence and severity of carditis and may be lifelong
Echocardiographic evidence of valvular involvement counts as carditis even without a clinical murmur
explanation.pearls: In high-risk populations in Australia, monoarthritis (not just polyarthritis) and polyarthralgia (not just polyarthritis) can each count as a major Jones criterion. IM benzathine penicillin G dose is 450 mg (600,000 units) for children under 20 kg and 900 mg (1.2 million units) for those 20 kg and above, administered every 28 days (not monthly). For ARF with carditis, secondary prophylaxis is recommended for a minimum of 10 years or until age 21, whichever is longer, and potentially lifelong if there is persistent valvular disease.

subtopic_detail: Septic arthritis vs transient synovitis - decision to aspirate
stem:
A 4-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents with a 1-day history of right hip pain and refusal to weight-bear. He had a mild upper respiratory tract infection 5 days ago that has since resolved. He has no significant past medical history and immunisations are up to date. On examination he is irritable and holds his right hip in flexion and external rotation.

He cries with any attempted passive range of motion of the right hip. There is no overlying erythema or swelling visible externally. His left hip has full painless range of motion.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 130, RR 24, Temp 38.5 degrees C, BP 95/60 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Right hip held in flexion and external rotation, cries with passive movement, unable to weight-bear, no skin changes
data_table.Investigations: WCC 14.2 x10^9/L, CRP 45 mg/L, ESR 32 mm/hr, blood cultures taken. Ultrasound right hip: moderate joint effusion

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence oral antibiotics and arrange clinical review in 24 hours
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics without joint aspiration are insufficient when septic arthritis is suspected. The diagnosis requires synovial fluid analysis to distinguish septic arthritis from transient synovitis. Additionally, if septic arthritis is confirmed, IV antibiotics and surgical washout are required rather than oral therapy. (Source: RCH CPG - Septic Arthritis)

options.text: Arrange MRI of the right hip before considering any intervention
options.rationale: MRI can help identify adjacent osteomyelitis or soft tissue abscess, but it should not delay joint aspiration when septic arthritis is the primary concern. Joint aspiration is both diagnostic and therapeutic and can be performed promptly under ultrasound guidance without needing MRI beforehand. (Source: eTG - Bone and Joint Infections)

options.text: Proceed to joint aspiration under ultrasound guidance promptly
options.rationale: This child scores highly on the Kocher criteria (fever, non-weight-bearing, elevated WCC, and elevated ESR), making septic arthritis the leading differential. Urgent ultrasound-guided joint aspiration is the key diagnostic step, providing synovial fluid for cell count, Gram stain, and culture. Aspiration also provides therapeutic decompression of the joint. (Source: RCH CPG - Septic Arthritis)

options.text: Trial a course of NSAIDs with bed rest and reassess in 48 hours
options.rationale: A trial of NSAIDs may be appropriate for transient synovitis where clinical suspicion of septic arthritis is low (afebrile, normal inflammatory markers). In this child with fever, elevated CRP and WCC, and significant hip guarding, delaying aspiration to trial conservative treatment risks progression of joint destruction from untreated septic arthritis. (Source: RCH CPG - Septic Arthritis)

options.text: Arrange a bone scan to exclude osteomyelitis before aspiration
options.rationale: A bone scan is not required before hip aspiration and would introduce unnecessary delay. Joint aspiration takes priority to confirm or exclude septic arthritis. If osteomyelitis is also suspected, MRI is the preferred imaging modality over bone scintigraphy due to superior anatomical detail. (Source: eTG - Bone and Joint Infections)

explanation.summary: This child presents with an acute irritable hip meeting multiple Kocher criteria (fever over 38.5 degrees C, refusal to weight-bear, WCC over 12 x10^9/L, ESR over 40 mm/hr) indicating a high probability of septic arthritis. The essential next step is urgent ultrasound-guided joint aspiration to obtain synovial fluid for cell count, Gram stain, and culture. Confirmed septic arthritis requires IV antibiotics and surgical washout to prevent cartilage destruction. Transient synovitis, in contrast, is managed with NSAIDs and observation.
key_points: Kocher criteria help distinguish septic arthritis from transient synovitis: fever, non-weight-bearing, WCC over 12 x10^9/L, ESR over 40 mm/hr
Meeting 3 or more Kocher criteria confers a greater than 90% probability of septic arthritis
Joint aspiration is both diagnostic and therapeutic and should not be delayed by imaging
Staphylococcus aureus is the most common organism in paediatric septic arthritis
explanation.pearls: The Kocher criteria predict the probability of septic arthritis: 1 criterion confers approximately 3% risk, 2 criteria approximately 40%, 3 criteria approximately 93%, and 4 criteria approximately 99%. CRP greater than 20 mg/L has been added as a fifth predictor. Synovial fluid WCC greater than 50,000 x10^6/L with more than 75% neutrophils is highly suggestive of septic arthritis. Empirical IV flucloxacillin (50 mg/kg every 6 hours, maximum 2 g) is first-line in children over 3 months pending culture results.

subtopic_detail: Slipped upper femoral epiphysis - management urgency
stem:
A 12-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with a 3-week history of progressive left hip pain and an intermittent limp. He also reports left knee pain which prompted the initial presentation to his general practitioner 2 weeks ago. His GP prescribed paracetamol and advised rest. The pain has worsened and he now has difficulty walking. He is overweight with a BMI on the 95th centile.

He is Tanner stage 2 for pubic hair. On examination he has an antalgic gait. His left hip has limited internal rotation, and when the hip is flexed it obligatorily externally rotates. There is no effusion. Plain radiographs are obtained.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 85, RR 18, Temp 36.7 degrees C, BP 115/72 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Antalgic gait, left hip limited internal rotation, obligatory external rotation with flexion, no effusion, BMI 95th centile
data_table.Investigations: AP pelvis X-ray: subtle widening of the left proximal femoral physis. Frog-leg lateral X-ray: posterior displacement of the left femoral epiphysis with a slip angle of approximately 35 degrees. Right hip: normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Commence physiotherapy and a structured weight loss program
options.rationale: Physiotherapy and weight loss are not appropriate management for slipped upper femoral epiphysis. SUFE requires surgical fixation to prevent further slip and complications such as avascular necrosis and chondrolysis. Weight-bearing and physical activity without stabilisation risks acute-on-chronic progression. (Source: RCH CPG - Slipped Upper Femoral Epiphysis)
source_refs: RCH CPG - Slipped Upper Femoral Epiphysis

options.text: Arrange MRI of the left hip for further characterisation first
options.rationale: MRI is not required when plain radiographs have already confirmed the diagnosis of SUFE with a visible slip on the frog-leg lateral view. MRI may be useful when X-rays are equivocal or to assess the contralateral hip, but in this case the diagnosis is established and delays to definitive surgical management should be avoided. (Source: eTG - Paediatric Orthopaedics)

options.text: Prescribe analgesia and arrange orthopaedic review in two weeks
options.rationale: Delayed orthopaedic review is inappropriate for confirmed SUFE. Without surgical stabilisation, the slip may progress acutely, increasing the risk of avascular necrosis of the femoral head. The child should be made non-weight-bearing immediately and referred urgently for in-situ pinning. (Source: RCH CPG - Slipped Upper Femoral Epiphysis)
source_refs: RCH CPG - Slipped Upper Femoral Epiphysis

options.text: Make non-weight-bearing and arrange urgent orthopaedic pinning
options.rationale: SUFE requires urgent orthopaedic management. The child should be made non-weight-bearing immediately (crutches or wheelchair) and referred for urgent in-situ percutaneous screw fixation to prevent further slip progression. The goal of surgery is to stabilise the epiphysis in its current position without attempting reduction, which carries a high risk of avascular necrosis. (Source: RCH CPG - Slipped Upper Femoral Epiphysis)
source_refs: RCH CPG - Slipped Upper Femoral Epiphysis

options.text: Request bilateral hip CT scan for detailed surgical planning
options.rationale: CT scanning is not routinely required for surgical planning in SUFE when plain radiographs clearly demonstrate the slip. CT exposes the child to significant radiation and does not change the immediate management decision. In-situ pinning is performed using intraoperative fluoroscopy rather than preoperative CT planning. (Source: eTG - Paediatric Orthopaedics)

explanation.summary: Slipped upper femoral epiphysis (SUFE) is a common adolescent hip condition where the femoral head displaces posteriorly and inferiorly through the growth plate. It typically affects overweight adolescents during periods of rapid growth. The hallmark clinical finding is obligatory external rotation of the hip with flexion. The frog-leg lateral radiograph is the most sensitive plain film view for detecting the slip. Management requires immediate non-weight-bearing status and urgent in-situ screw fixation to prevent further displacement.
key_points: Referred knee pain is a common initial presentation that can delay diagnosis of SUFE
Obligatory external rotation with hip flexion is the classic examination finding
The frog-leg lateral X-ray view is more sensitive than the AP view for detecting slips
In-situ fixation without reduction is the standard surgical approach to minimise avascular necrosis risk
explanation.pearls: SUFE is classified as stable (able to weight-bear) or unstable (unable to weight-bear), with unstable SUFE carrying a 20 to 50% risk of avascular necrosis compared with less than 5% for stable slips. In-situ pinning with a single cannulated screw is the standard treatment for stable SUFE. Prophylactic pinning of the contralateral hip is considered in patients with endocrine disorders or those younger than 10 years, where the risk of bilateral SUFE is highest. All children with SUFE should be screened for hypothyroidism and other endocrinopathies.

sources.label: RCH CPG - Slipped Upper Femoral Epiphysis

subtopic_detail: Orbital cellulitis vs preseptal cellulitis - distinguishing features and management
stem:
A 5-year-old girl presents to the emergency department with progressive left periorbital swelling and redness over the past 2 days. She had been treated by her GP for acute sinusitis with oral amoxicillin for 5 days with minimal improvement. Over the past 12 hours her parents have noticed that her left eye appears to be bulging forward and she is complaining of pain when looking to the side.

She has had fevers to 39.2 degrees C. On examination there is marked left periorbital oedema and erythema. The left eye is proptotic with 3 mm of axial displacement. Extraocular movements are restricted in all directions with pain on attempted lateral gaze. Visual acuity is reduced in the left eye compared with the right. The right eye is normal.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 125, RR 24, Temp 39.0 degrees C, BP 100/62 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Left periorbital oedema and erythema, 3 mm proptosis, restricted extraocular movements in all directions, pain with eye movement, reduced visual acuity left eye, right eye normal, no relative afferent pupillary defect
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18.5 x10^9/L, CRP 120 mg/L, blood cultures taken. CT orbits with contrast: left medial subperiosteal abscess measuring 12 x 8 mm with adjacent ethmoid sinusitis, no intracranial extension

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Switch to oral amoxicillin-clavulanate and review as outpatient
options.rationale: Outpatient oral antibiotics are appropriate for preseptal cellulitis but not for orbital cellulitis with a subperiosteal abscess. This child has postseptal disease with proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, and reduced visual acuity requiring IV antibiotics and likely surgical drainage. Outpatient management risks vision loss and intracranial complications. (Source: RCH CPG - Orbital Cellulitis)

options.text: Commence IV antibiotics alone and reassess at 48 hours for response
options.rationale: While IV antibiotics are necessary, a 12 mm subperiosteal abscess with restricted eye movements and reduced visual acuity warrants urgent surgical drainage rather than a trial of antibiotics alone. Delaying drainage in the presence of visual compromise risks permanent vision loss from optic nerve compression or central retinal artery occlusion. (Source: eTG - Eye Infections)

options.text: Prescribe topical chloramphenicol and oral antibiotics concurrently
options.rationale: Topical antibiotics do not penetrate to the orbit and are ineffective for postseptal infections. Orbital cellulitis with a subperiosteal abscess requires systemic IV antibiotics with appropriate central nervous system penetration and surgical drainage of the abscess collection. (Source: RCH CPG - Orbital Cellulitis)

options.text: Perform incision and drainage under local anaesthesia at bedside
options.rationale: Bedside incision and drainage under local anaesthesia is not appropriate for a subperiosteal orbital abscess in a child. Drainage requires a formal surgical approach, typically endoscopic sinus surgery with drainage performed under general anaesthesia by an ENT surgeon or combined ENT and ophthalmology team. (Source: eTG - Eye Infections)

options.text: Commence IV antibiotics and arrange urgent specialist surgical review
options.rationale: This child has orbital cellulitis with a subperiosteal abscess, proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, and reduced visual acuity - all features indicating postseptal disease requiring urgent intervention. Management includes IV antibiotics (typically flucloxacillin and ceftriaxone or piperacillin-tazobactam) and urgent ophthalmology and ENT review for drainage of the abscess. (Source: RCH CPG - Orbital Cellulitis)

explanation.summary: This child has orbital (postseptal) cellulitis complicated by a subperiosteal abscess secondary to ethmoid sinusitis. The distinguishing features from preseptal cellulitis include proptosis, pain with eye movements, restricted extraocular movements, and reduced visual acuity. CT with contrast confirms the subperiosteal abscess. Management requires IV antibiotics and urgent surgical drainage by ophthalmology and ENT to prevent vision-threatening complications including optic nerve compression and cavernous sinus thrombosis.
key_points: Proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, and pain with eye movements distinguish orbital from preseptal cellulitis
CT orbits with contrast is the imaging modality of choice to identify abscess and assess for intracranial extension
IV antibiotics plus surgical drainage are required for subperiosteal abscess with visual compromise
Ethmoid sinusitis is the most common predisposing factor for orbital cellulitis in children
explanation.pearls: Indications for surgical drainage of a subperiosteal abscess include abscess size greater than 10 mm, reduced visual acuity, a relative afferent pupillary defect, or failure to improve after 24 to 48 hours of IV antibiotics. First-line IV antibiotic regimens at RCH include ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg (maximum 2 g) daily plus flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg (maximum 2 g) every 6 hours, or piperacillin-tazobactam 100 mg/kg (maximum 4 g) every 8 hours. Metronidazole should be added if anaerobic infection or intracranial extension is suspected. Cavernous sinus thrombosis is a life-threatening complication indicated by bilateral eye signs, cranial nerve palsies, and altered mental status.

subtopic_detail: Staggered paracetamol overdose - NAC decision
stem: A 14-year-old girl presents to the emergency department reporting that she took approximately 10 g of paracetamol in divided doses over an 8-hour period following an argument with her parents. She took the first dose at 6 pm and the last dose at 2 am. She presents at 4 am (10 hours after the first dose). She has no symptoms of nausea or vomiting. She takes no regular medications and has no significant past medical history.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 82 bpm, BP 110/68 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99% on room air, Temp 36.8 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Alert and oriented, abdomen soft and non-tender, no jaundice, no hepatomegaly
data_table.Investigations: ALT 22 U/L (ref <40), INR 1.0, creatinine 55 micromol/L, serum paracetamol 450 micromol/L at 10 hours post first ingestion

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence intravenous N-acetylcysteine infusion immediately
options.rationale: In staggered paracetamol overdoses (doses taken over more than 1 hour), the Rumack-Matthew nomogram cannot be used to guide treatment as it is only validated for single acute ingestions. Australian toxicology guidelines recommend commencing NAC in all staggered ingestions where the total dose exceeds 200 mg/kg or 10 g (whichever is less) over any 24-hour period. This patient ingested 10 g over 8 hours, meeting this threshold, and NAC should be started regardless of the serum paracetamol level. (Source: Austin Health Toxicology Guidelines 2024)

options.text: Plot the paracetamol level on the Rumack-Matthew nomogram
options.rationale: The Rumack-Matthew nomogram is only validated for single acute ingestions taken at a known time point. In staggered overdoses where paracetamol has been taken over more than 1 hour, the nomogram cannot reliably predict hepatotoxicity risk, and treatment decisions must be made on clinical grounds and total dose ingested. (Source: Austin Health Toxicology Guidelines 2024)

options.text: Administer activated charcoal via nasogastric tube promptly
options.rationale: Activated charcoal is most effective within 1-2 hours of ingestion and has limited benefit beyond this window. At 10 hours post first ingestion and with the last dose taken 2 hours ago, activated charcoal would have minimal benefit for the earlier doses and questionable benefit for the last dose. NAC is the priority treatment. (Source: eTG Toxicology 2024)

options.text: Send bloods and defer treatment pending ALT trend results
options.rationale: Waiting for ALT results before commencing NAC in a staggered overdose is inappropriate. ALT elevation is a late marker of hepatotoxicity, and treatment should not be delayed while awaiting serial results. NAC is most effective when commenced early, ideally within 8 hours of ingestion, and delay worsens outcomes. (Source: Austin Health Toxicology Guidelines 2024)

options.text: Observe with serial bloods for a further 8-hour period
options.rationale: Observation alone is not appropriate for a staggered paracetamol overdose exceeding the treatment threshold. Unlike single acute ingestions where the nomogram can guide a decision to observe, staggered ingestions require empiric NAC treatment when the total dose exceeds 200 mg/kg or 10 g. Delay risks hepatotoxicity. (Source: eTG Toxicology 2024)

explanation.summary: Staggered paracetamol overdoses cannot be assessed using the Rumack-Matthew nomogram, which is only validated for single acute ingestions at a known time point. Australian guidelines recommend commencing NAC for all staggered ingestions where the total dose exceeds 200 mg/kg or 10 g (whichever is less) over a 24-hour period. In this case, the 10 g total dose clearly exceeds the treatment threshold and NAC should be commenced regardless of the serum paracetamol level or current liver function results. Early NAC administration is most effective when commenced within 8 hours of ingestion.
key_points: The Rumack-Matthew nomogram is only valid for single acute paracetamol ingestions at a known time
Staggered overdose is defined as ingestion over more than 1 hour
NAC treatment threshold for staggered overdose is 200 mg/kg or 10 g total over 24 hours
NAC is most effective when commenced within 8 hours of the first ingestion dose
explanation.pearls: For staggered paracetamol overdoses in children and adolescents, the NAC treatment threshold is 200 mg/kg or 10 g (whichever is less) taken over any 24-hour period. The standard 2-bag NAC protocol (200 mg/kg over 4 hours then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours) is used in Australia. Anaphylactoid reactions to NAC are common (10-20%) and managed with temporary infusion cessation and antihistamines, not by stopping NAC permanently.

subtopic_detail: Button battery lodged in oesophagus - urgency
stem: A 2-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents who witnessed him swallow a coin-shaped object from a remote control approximately 2 hours ago. He has been drooling excessively and refusing to eat or drink since the ingestion. He has had one episode of non-bilious vomiting. He has no significant past medical history and is up to date with immunisations.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 130 bpm, BP 90/55 mmHg, RR 28, SpO2 98% on room air, Temp 37.1 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Drooling, distressed, no stridor, no subcutaneous emphysema, abdomen soft and non-tender
data_table.Investigations: Chest X-ray shows a round radio-opaque foreign body at the level of the mid-oesophagus with a double-ring (halo) sign on anteroposterior view and step-off on lateral view

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Observe with repeat chest X-ray in 24 hours for passage
options.rationale: Observation is not appropriate for a button battery lodged in the oesophagus. Tissue injury from the electrical current generated by the battery begins within 15-30 minutes of mucosal contact and can cause full-thickness oesophageal burns, perforation, and fatal aorto-oesophageal fistula within 2 hours. Delay in removal significantly increases morbidity and mortality. (Source: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Button Battery Ingestion 2024)

options.text: Arrange urgent endoscopic removal within 2 hours of ingestion
options.rationale: A button battery lodged in the oesophagus is a time-critical surgical emergency. Australian guidelines recommend endoscopic removal within 2 hours of ingestion (or as soon as possible if beyond 2 hours). The double-ring sign on X-ray confirms this is a button battery rather than a coin. Oesophageal button batteries cause liquefactive necrosis through generation of an external current, leading to burns, perforation, and potentially fatal complications. (Source: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Button Battery Ingestion 2024)

options.text: Administer oral honey every 10 minutes to reduce injury
options.rationale: While honey has been shown in animal studies to reduce oesophageal injury from button batteries by creating a viscous protective barrier, it is an adjunctive measure only and should never delay definitive endoscopic removal. Honey is also contraindicated in children under 12 months due to botulism risk. In this case, definitive removal is the priority. (Source: National Capital Poison Center - Button Battery Guidelines 2023)

options.text: Administer intravenous glucagon to promote distal passage
options.rationale: Glucagon to promote oesophageal relaxation and distal passage is not recommended for button batteries. Unlike food bolus impaction, allowing a button battery to pass distally does not eliminate the ongoing tissue injury while it remains in the oesophagus. The priority is rapid endoscopic removal, as any delay increases the risk of oesophageal necrosis and perforation. (Source: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Button Battery Ingestion 2024)

options.text: Attempt advancement into stomach using a nasogastric tube
options.rationale: Blind advancement of a button battery using a nasogastric tube is not recommended. This approach risks oesophageal perforation, particularly if tissue necrosis has already commenced. The battery must be removed under direct vision via endoscopy to allow assessment of the oesophageal mucosa for injury and to plan further management accordingly. (Source: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Button Battery Ingestion 2024)

explanation.summary: A button battery lodged in the oesophagus is a time-critical emergency requiring endoscopic removal within 2 hours. The double-ring (halo) sign on X-ray differentiates a button battery from a coin. Button batteries cause liquefactive necrosis through generation of an external electrical current at the negative pole, which can cause full-thickness oesophageal burns within 2 hours. Fatal aorto-oesophageal fistula is the most feared complication and can occur days after the battery has been removed.
key_points: The double-ring sign on AP X-ray and step-off on lateral view differentiate button batteries from coins
Oesophageal button battery impaction requires endoscopic removal within 2 hours
Tissue injury begins within 15-30 minutes of mucosal contact via electrical current
Honey is an adjunct only and must not delay definitive removal
explanation.pearls: Lithium button batteries 20 mm or larger are most commonly implicated in severe oesophageal injuries. The negative pole of the battery (the flat side) contacts the oesophageal mucosa and generates hydroxide ions via electrolysis, causing liquefactive necrosis. Post-removal, children require observation for at least 24 hours as delayed perforation and aorto-oesophageal fistula can occur up to 28 days after removal.

subtopic_detail: Iron poisoning - deferoxamine threshold
stem: A 3-year-old girl (weight 14 kg) is brought to the emergency department by ambulance 2 hours after her mother found her with an open bottle of ferrous sulfate tablets. The mother estimates approximately 20 tablets (325 mg ferrous sulfate each, 65 mg elemental iron per tablet) may have been ingested, equating to approximately 93 mg/kg elemental iron. The child has vomited four times with blood-stained vomitus and is complaining of abdominal pain. She had one loose stool prior to arrival.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 150 bpm, BP 85/50 mmHg, RR 32, SpO2 97% on room air, Temp 37.4 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Lethargic, pale, tender abdomen diffusely, no peritonism, capillary refill 3 seconds peripherally
data_table.Investigations: Serum iron 95 micromol/L at 4 hours post-ingestion (ref 9-30), WCC 18.2 x10^9/L, glucose 12.5 mmol/L, venous pH 7.28, lactate 4.5 mmol/L, AXR shows multiple radio-opaque tablets in stomach and proximal small bowel

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Administer activated charcoal and observe with serial bloods
options.rationale: Activated charcoal does not bind iron and is ineffective in iron poisoning. Iron is not adsorbed by charcoal due to its ionic nature, so this intervention would provide no benefit and may delay definitive treatment with deferoxamine. The clinical features and serum iron level indicate severe iron toxicity requiring chelation therapy. (Source: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Iron Poisoning 2024)

options.text: Arrange gastric lavage under general anaesthesia urgently
options.rationale: Gastric lavage is generally not recommended in iron poisoning as iron tablets may be too large to pass through the lavage tube. Additionally, lavage does not remove tablets that have passed beyond the pylorus, as shown on this patient's AXR. Whole bowel irrigation with polyethylene glycol solution is the preferred decontamination method for iron tablets visible on AXR. (Source: eTG Toxicology 2024)

options.text: Commence IV deferoxamine infusion and whole bowel irrigation
options.rationale: This patient has severe iron poisoning with a serum iron level of 95 micromol/L (well above the 90 micromol/L threshold indicating severe toxicity), metabolic acidosis, and systemic symptoms. IV deferoxamine (15 mg/kg/hour, maximum 80 mg/kg/day) is indicated for serum iron above 90 micromol/L, significant metabolic acidosis, or haemodynamic instability. Whole bowel irrigation with polyethylene glycol solution is indicated as AXR shows retained tablets in the GI tract. (Source: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Iron Poisoning 2024)

options.text: Commence IV fluid resuscitation and supportive care alone
options.rationale: While IV fluid resuscitation is an important component of management in iron poisoning with haemodynamic compromise, supportive care alone is insufficient when the serum iron level exceeds 90 micromol/L with metabolic acidosis. This patient meets criteria for chelation therapy with deferoxamine, which binds free iron to form ferrioxamine that is renally excreted. (Source: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Iron Poisoning 2024)

options.text: Administer oral desferrioxamine via nasogastric tube now
options.rationale: Oral or intragastric desferrioxamine is not recommended in iron poisoning. This route of administration is ineffective for chelation as deferoxamine must reach the systemic circulation to bind free serum iron. Previously proposed gastric lavage with deferoxamine solution has been abandoned as it does not improve outcomes and may worsen hypotension. (Source: eTG Toxicology 2024)

explanation.summary: Severe iron poisoning requires IV deferoxamine chelation therapy when the serum iron level exceeds 90 micromol/L, there is significant metabolic acidosis, or the patient is haemodynamically unstable. Activated charcoal does not bind iron and is not useful. When radio-opaque tablets are visible on AXR, whole bowel irrigation with polyethylene glycol solution should be commenced concurrently to reduce ongoing absorption. Deferoxamine is administered as a continuous IV infusion at 15 mg/kg/hour.
key_points: Activated charcoal does not bind iron and is ineffective in iron poisoning
IV deferoxamine is indicated when serum iron exceeds 90 micromol/L or there is metabolic acidosis
Whole bowel irrigation is the preferred GI decontamination method for iron tablets
Deferoxamine infusion rate is 15 mg/kg/hour up to a maximum of 80 mg/kg/day
explanation.pearls: Deferoxamine at 15 mg/kg/hour IV (max 80 mg/kg/day) is commenced when serum iron exceeds 90 micromol/L at 4-6 hours post-ingestion. The vin rose (pinkish-red) colour of urine during deferoxamine therapy indicates ferrioxamine excretion but is not a reliable marker of chelation adequacy. Deferoxamine infusion for more than 24 hours increases the risk of acute respiratory distress syndrome, so duration should be minimised.

subtopic_detail: Skeletal survey findings concerning for NAI
stem: An 8-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents because he has not been moving his left arm since this morning. The parents state he rolled off the change table onto carpet from a height of approximately 60 cm yesterday afternoon. He was born at term with no neonatal complications. He is formula-fed and growing along the 50th centile. There is no family history of bone disease. A skeletal survey has been performed.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 140 bpm, BP 80/50 mmHg, RR 30, SpO2 99% on room air, Temp 36.9 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Distressed when left arm is moved, swelling over mid-left humerus, no bruising on trunk, normal fontanelle, blue sclerae absent, teeth normal
data_table.Investigations: Left humerus X-ray: spiral fracture of the mid-shaft. Skeletal survey: healing posterior rib fractures of varying ages bilaterally (ribs 6-8 left, ribs 7-9 right), classic metaphyseal lesion (bucket-handle pattern) of the left distal tibia. FBC, UEC, calcium, phosphate, ALP, and vitamin D all within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Accept the parental explanation and arrange outpatient review
options.rationale: The combination of a spiral humerus fracture in a pre-ambulatory infant, posterior rib fractures of different ages, and a classic metaphyseal lesion is highly suspicious for non-accidental injury. A fall from 60 cm onto carpet would not explain multiple fractures of varying ages. Accepting this explanation without further investigation would place the child at ongoing risk of harm. (Source: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Child Abuse 2024)

options.text: Request bone density testing to exclude osteogenesis imperfecta
options.rationale: While osteogenesis imperfecta (OI) is an important differential for multiple fractures, this child lacks clinical features of OI (no blue sclerae, normal teeth, no family history, normal growth). Classic metaphyseal lesions are virtually pathognomonic for non-accidental injury and are not a feature of OI. Bone density testing should not delay mandatory reporting and child protection involvement. (Source: RACP Position Statement - Child Abuse 2023)

options.text: Obtain MRI of the left humerus for further characterisation
options.rationale: MRI of the humerus would not change the immediate management. The plain radiograph findings are sufficient to characterise the spiral fracture, and the skeletal survey has already identified additional fractures highly concerning for non-accidental injury. The priority is ensuring child safety through notification and multidisciplinary assessment rather than further imaging. (Source: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Child Abuse 2024)

options.text: Notify child protection authorities and admit for assessment
options.rationale: The fracture pattern is highly suspicious for non-accidental injury. Posterior rib fractures in infants result from anteroposterior compression (squeezing) and are strongly associated with abuse. Classic metaphyseal lesions occur from shearing forces at the growth plate. Multiple fractures of different ages in a pre-ambulatory infant with an inadequate mechanism are a mandatory notification trigger. The child must be admitted and a multidisciplinary assessment convened. (Source: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Child Abuse 2024)

options.text: Arrange genetic testing before involving child protection teams
options.rationale: Genetic testing for collagen disorders should not delay mandatory notification to child protection services. In Australia, medical practitioners are mandatory reporters and must notify when they have reasonable grounds to suspect a child is at risk of significant harm. Investigation for alternative diagnoses can proceed concurrently but must not be used to defer protective action. (Source: RACP Position Statement - Child Abuse 2023)

explanation.summary: This presentation is highly concerning for non-accidental injury. The combination of a spiral humerus fracture in a pre-ambulatory infant, bilateral posterior rib fractures of varying ages, and a classic metaphyseal lesion (bucket-handle fracture) constitutes a fracture pattern strongly associated with physical abuse. The provided mechanism (fall from change table onto carpet) does not adequately explain these findings. Mandatory notification and admission for multidisciplinary assessment are required.
key_points: Posterior rib fractures in infants are highly specific for non-accidental injury
Classic metaphyseal lesions result from shearing forces and are virtually pathognomonic for abuse
Multiple fractures of different ages in a pre-ambulatory child strongly suggest repeated injury
Medical practitioners in Australia are mandatory reporters for suspected child abuse
explanation.pearls: Posterior rib fractures in infants have a specificity of over 95% for non-accidental injury and result from anteroposterior thoracic compression. Classic metaphyseal lesions (bucket-handle or corner fractures) occur at the growth plate from traction and torsional forces. A follow-up skeletal survey at 11-14 days is recommended as healing fractures become more visible on repeat imaging and may reveal additional injuries not seen on the initial survey.

subtopic_detail: Fabricated or induced illness - recognition and response
stem: A 6-year-old girl has been admitted to hospital 12 times over the past 2 years for reported generalised tonic-clonic seizures. Each episode is described in detail by her mother, who is a registered nurse. No seizure activity has ever been witnessed by hospital staff despite multiple admissions. The child is developmentally normal but has become increasingly anxious about medical procedures and hospital visits. Nursing staff have observed that reported events only occur when the mother is present at the bedside.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 90 bpm, BP 100/60 mmHg, RR 20, SpO2 99% on room air, Temp 36.7 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Well-appearing child, neurologically intact, no focal deficits, anxious demeanour, multiple venepuncture scars on upper limbs
data_table.Investigations: EEG (x3): normal. MRI brain: normal. Serum prolactin post-reported events: within normal limits. All metabolic screening: normal. Full blood count and biochemistry: normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Trial anticonvulsant medication to assess treatment response
options.rationale: Commencing anticonvulsant medication empirically without a confirmed diagnosis of epilepsy would expose this child to unnecessary drug side effects. Multiple normal EEGs, normal post-ictal prolactin levels, and the absence of witnessed events by any medical professional strongly argue against a seizure disorder. Treating an unconfirmed condition may reinforce the illness narrative. (Source: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Fabricated or Induced Illness 2024)

options.text: Request prolonged inpatient video-EEG telemetry monitoring
options.rationale: While video-EEG telemetry can capture events, three previous EEGs have been normal and no seizure activity has been witnessed by staff. Further investigation without addressing the pattern of fabricated illness risks continued harm to the child through invasive procedures and prolonged hospitalisation. The clinical pattern strongly suggests fabricated illness rather than an undiagnosed seizure disorder. (Source: RACP Position Statement - Child Protection 2023)

options.text: Confront the mother directly with fabrication suspicions
options.rationale: Directly confronting the caregiver suspected of fabricating illness is not recommended as an initial step. This approach may cause the perpetrator to escalate harmful behaviours, seek care elsewhere, or flee with the child. A coordinated multiagency approach is needed to ensure child safety before any confrontation occurs, with input from child protection and mental health professionals. (Source: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Fabricated or Induced Illness 2024)

options.text: Discharge home with outpatient neurology follow-up only
options.rationale: Discharging with outpatient neurology follow-up alone fails to address the safeguarding concern. The pattern of repeated unnecessary admissions, investigations, and the child's increasing anxiety about medical procedures constitutes significant harm. Ongoing outpatient neurology review without child protection involvement risks continuation of the fabricated illness and escalation of harmful medical interventions. (Source: RACP Position Statement - Child Protection 2023)

options.text: Convene a multidisciplinary strategy meeting with child protection
options.rationale: The clinical presentation is consistent with fabricated or induced illness (formerly Munchausen syndrome by proxy). The appropriate response is to convene a multidisciplinary strategy meeting involving child protection services, the treating medical team, nursing, social work, and relevant specialists. This coordinated approach ensures child safety while planning the investigation and management in a structured manner. (Source: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Fabricated or Induced Illness 2024)

explanation.summary: Fabricated or induced illness should be suspected when a child has multiple unexplained presentations, investigations are consistently normal, events are only reported by one caregiver and never witnessed by staff, and the caregiver has medical knowledge that enables detailed symptom fabrication. The appropriate response is a coordinated multidisciplinary strategy meeting involving child protection services rather than direct confrontation or further unnecessary investigation. The child's developing anxiety about medical procedures represents ongoing harm that must be addressed.
key_points: Fabricated illness should be suspected when events are never witnessed by staff and all investigations are normal
Direct confrontation of the suspected perpetrator is not recommended as a first step
A multidisciplinary strategy meeting is the recommended initial response
The child's anxiety about medical procedures represents significant ongoing harm
explanation.pearls: In suspected fabricated or induced illness, post-ictal serum prolactin levels are typically elevated 10-20 minutes after a genuine generalised tonic-clonic seizure. Normal prolactin levels post-reported events support the absence of true seizure activity. The perpetrator is the biological mother in approximately 90% of cases, and medical knowledge is a recognised risk factor. A formal multiagency strategy meeting should occur before any disclosure to the suspected perpetrator.

subtopic_detail: CMPA presenting as non-IgE mediated food protein-induced enterocolitis
stem: A 4-month-old boy who is exclusively breastfed is brought to his general practitioner with a 6-week history of chronic loose stools containing mucus and occasional blood streaks. He is irritable for 2-4 hours after feeds but settles between episodes. He has mild perioral eczema. His weight has tracked along the 40th centile since birth with no faltering. He was born at term via normal vaginal delivery and has no other medical history. His mother eats a regular diet including dairy products.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 130 bpm, RR 34, SpO2 99% on room air, Temp 36.8 degrees C, weight 6.8 kg (40th centile)
data_table.Examination: Well-appearing, mild perioral eczema, abdomen soft with active bowel sounds, no hepatosplenomegaly, normal perianal examination, nappy shows mucousy stool with small blood streak
data_table.Investigations: Skin prick test to cow's milk: negative (wheal 0 mm). Specific IgE to cow's milk: less than 0.10 kU/L. FBC: mild eosinophilia (eosinophils 0.8 x10^9/L)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence maternal cow's milk protein elimination for 2-4 weeks
options.rationale: This presentation is consistent with non-IgE mediated cow's milk protein allergy in a breastfed infant. Negative skin prick test and specific IgE do not exclude non-IgE mediated allergy, which is a cell-mediated process. Australian guidelines recommend a maternal elimination diet excluding all cow's milk protein for 2-4 weeks while continuing breastfeeding. If symptoms resolve, a supervised reintroduction confirms the diagnosis. (Source: ASCIA Cow's Milk Allergy Guidelines 2024)

options.text: Switch from breastfeeding to a soy-based infant formula immediately
options.rationale: Soy formula is not recommended as a first-line alternative in infants under 6 months with suspected cow's milk protein allergy. Up to 50% of infants with CMPA will also react to soy protein due to cross-reactivity. Furthermore, discontinuing breastfeeding should be avoided when possible, as maternal dietary elimination is preferred to maintain the benefits of breast milk. (Source: ASCIA Cow's Milk Allergy Guidelines 2024)

options.text: Commence an extensively hydrolysed formula and cease breastfeeds
options.rationale: Extensively hydrolysed formula is the first-line formula alternative when breastfeeding is not possible in non-IgE mediated CMPA. However, the priority in a thriving breastfed infant is to trial a maternal elimination diet first rather than cease breastfeeding. Breastfeeding provides immunological and nutritional benefits that should be preserved where feasible. (Source: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - CMPA 2024)

options.text: Trial a proton pump inhibitor as initial empiric therapy
options.rationale: While irritability after feeds can be a feature of gastro-oesophageal reflux disease, the presence of mucousy bloody stools, mild eosinophilia, and perioral eczema are more consistent with non-IgE mediated CMPA. Proton pump inhibitors would not address the underlying allergic process and are not recommended as empiric therapy in infants without confirmed acid-related disease. (Source: ASCIA Cow's Milk Allergy Guidelines 2024)

options.text: Arrange upper and lower endoscopy with mucosal biopsy samples
options.rationale: Endoscopy with biopsy is not required as a first-line investigation in a thriving infant with suspected non-IgE mediated CMPA. The diagnosis is made clinically based on symptom improvement with cow's milk elimination and recurrence on reintroduction. Endoscopy may be considered in cases with faltering growth, severe symptoms, or failure to respond to an adequate elimination diet trial. (Source: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - CMPA 2024)

explanation.summary: Non-IgE mediated cow's milk protein allergy presents with delayed symptoms (2-72 hours after ingestion) including mucousy or bloody stools, irritability, and eczema. Skin prick tests and specific IgE are negative as the mechanism is cell-mediated rather than IgE-mediated. In exclusively breastfed infants, the first-line approach is a maternal cow's milk protein elimination diet for 2-4 weeks, with continued breastfeeding. Symptom resolution with elimination and recurrence on reintroduction confirms the diagnosis.
key_points: Negative skin prick test and specific IgE do not exclude non-IgE mediated CMPA
Maternal elimination diet for 2-4 weeks is first line in breastfed infants
Soy formula is not recommended under 6 months due to high cross-reactivity rates
Diagnosis is confirmed by symptom resolution on elimination and recurrence on rechallenge
explanation.pearls: Non-IgE mediated CMPA symptoms typically appear 2-72 hours after cow's milk protein exposure, distinguishing it from IgE-mediated allergy (minutes to 2 hours). Up to 50% of infants with CMPA also react to soy protein. Maternal elimination diets require complete avoidance of all dairy including hidden sources (casein, whey in processed foods). Most children with non-IgE mediated CMPA develop tolerance by 3 years of age, and supervised reintroduction using the MAP milk ladder is recommended at 9-12 months intervals.

subtopic_detail: Anaphylaxis action plan - adrenaline device prescription
stem:
A 5-year-old boy (weighing 22 kg) is recovering in the emergency department following an episode of anaphylaxis after eating a biscuit containing peanut at a birthday party. He developed generalised urticaria, then vomiting, audible wheeze, and became pale and floppy. Paramedics administered intramuscular adrenaline 150 mcg and he responded well. In the emergency department, he received a further dose of intramuscular adrenaline 150 mcg for recurrent wheeze 20 minutes later.

He has been stable for 4 hours with no further symptoms. Specific IgE to peanut returns at 28 kU/L. He has a background of mild eczema but no prior allergic reactions to foods.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 100 bpm, BP 100/65 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 98% on room air, Temp 36.9 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Well-appearing, chest clear, no urticaria, no angioedema, well perfused peripherally
data_table.Investigations: Serum tryptase (drawn at 1 hour): 18 mcg/L (ref <11.5). Specific IgE peanut: 28 kU/L (strongly positive). FBC and UEC: normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate discharge plan for this patient?

options.text: Prescribe adrenaline autoinjector 300 mcg device with an action plan
options.rationale: The 300 mcg adrenaline autoinjector is recommended for children weighing 20 kg or more by some international guidelines, but the ASCIA guidelines recommend the 150 mcg device for children aged 1-5 years weighing 7.5-20 kg, and the 300 mcg device for children over 5 years or weighing over 20 kg. However, ASCIA guidelines specify the 150 mcg device is appropriate for children up to 20 kg and transition to 300 mcg typically occurs around age 5-6 years. At 22 kg, either dose could be considered, but the key error here is the omission of training and written action plan components described in option B. (Source: ASCIA Action Plan for Anaphylaxis 2024)

options.text: Prescribe adrenaline autoinjector 150 mcg with action plan and training
options.rationale: ASCIA recommends the 150 mcg adrenaline autoinjector for children aged 1-5 years. At 5 years and 22 kg, the 150 mcg device remains appropriate. The comprehensive discharge plan must include prescription of the device, a personalised ASCIA Action Plan for Anaphylaxis, hands-on device training for the family, allergen avoidance education, and referral to a paediatric allergist. Medical alert identification should also be discussed. (Source: ASCIA Action Plan for Anaphylaxis 2024)

options.text: Prescribe oral cetirizine and salbutamol inhaler for acute events
options.rationale: Antihistamines and bronchodilators alone are insufficient for managing anaphylaxis. Antihistamines treat urticaria and itch but do not reverse the cardiovascular or respiratory compromise of anaphylaxis. Salbutamol may help with bronchospasm but does not treat hypotension or angioedema. Adrenaline is the only first-line treatment for anaphylaxis and must be prescribed. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines 2024)

options.text: Arrange peanut oral immunotherapy desensitisation before discharge
options.rationale: Peanut oral immunotherapy is a specialist-supervised intervention that requires careful patient selection, gradual dose escalation over months, and close monitoring. It is not initiated during an acute admission and certainly not before discharge from an anaphylaxis episode. Referral to a paediatric allergist for consideration of immunotherapy is appropriate as an outpatient. (Source: ASCIA Peanut Allergy Guidelines 2024)

options.text: Prescribe a 3-day oral prednisolone course and arrange GP review
options.rationale: Oral corticosteroids may be considered as an adjunctive treatment to prevent biphasic anaphylaxis, but they are not a substitute for adrenaline autoinjector prescription. The most critical component of the discharge plan is ensuring the patient has access to adrenaline for future episodes, a written action plan, and appropriate training. Steroid courses alone do not address future anaphylaxis risk. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines 2024)

explanation.summary: Following anaphylaxis, the essential discharge components include prescription of an adrenaline autoinjector at the appropriate dose, a personalised ASCIA Action Plan for Anaphylaxis, hands-on device training for the child and caregivers, allergen avoidance counselling, and referral to a paediatric allergist. ASCIA recommends the 150 mcg device for children aged 1-5 years. Antihistamines and bronchodilators are adjunctive treatments only and do not replace adrenaline for anaphylaxis management.
key_points: ASCIA recommends the 150 mcg adrenaline autoinjector for children aged 1-5 years
Discharge planning must include device prescription, written action plan, and hands-on training
Antihistamines and bronchodilators alone are insufficient for anaphylaxis management
Referral to a paediatric allergist is required for ongoing management
explanation.pearls: ASCIA prescribes the adrenaline autoinjector 150 mcg for children aged 1-5 years (approximately 7.5-20 kg) and the 300 mcg device for children over 5 years or weighing more than 20 kg. Each patient should be prescribed two adrenaline autoinjector devices. Serum tryptase drawn 1-2 hours after symptom onset supports the diagnosis of anaphylaxis when elevated above 11.5 mcg/L, and a baseline tryptase should be drawn at least 24 hours later to exclude mastocytosis.

subtopic_detail: Leukocoria in infant - retinoblastoma workup
stem: A 9-month-old girl is brought to the general practitioner by her mother who has noticed a white glow in her daughter's left eye in photographs taken over the past 2 months. The child is otherwise well, feeding normally, and reaching developmental milestones. She was born at 38 weeks gestation with no neonatal complications. There is no family history of eye cancer or genetic conditions. On examination, the red reflex is absent in the left eye and present in the right eye. Visual tracking appears normal bilaterally.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 128 bpm, RR 30, SpO2 99% on room air, Temp 36.8 degrees C, weight 8.5 kg (50th centile)
data_table.Examination: Well-appearing infant, anterior segments of both eyes appear normal, left pupil shows white reflex on direct ophthalmoscopy, right red reflex present and normal, no proptosis, no periorbital swelling
data_table.Investigations: No investigations performed prior to presentation

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Arrange routine ophthalmology outpatient review within 4 weeks
options.rationale: An absent red reflex with leukocoria in an infant requires urgent assessment, not routine review. Retinoblastoma is the most important diagnosis to exclude, and delayed diagnosis significantly impacts prognosis, including risk of extraocular extension, metastasis, and loss of the eye. Australian guidelines recommend same-day or next-day ophthalmology assessment for leukocoria. (Source: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Red Reflex Testing 2024)

options.text: Commence empiric topical chloramphenicol for possible infection
options.rationale: Leukocoria (white pupillary reflex) is not consistent with a simple eye infection. While endophthalmitis can cause leukocoria, it presents with a red, painful eye and systemic illness, which are absent here. The chronic nature of the white reflex over 2 months and absence of inflammatory signs make infection unlikely. The priority is urgent ophthalmological assessment to exclude retinoblastoma. (Source: COSA Retinoblastoma Guidelines 2023)

options.text: Arrange urgent ophthalmology examination under anaesthesia and MRI
options.rationale: Leukocoria with an absent red reflex in an infant requires urgent ophthalmological assessment. Examination under anaesthesia allows detailed fundoscopy, measurement of any tumour, and assessment of both eyes. MRI of the orbits and brain (not CT) is the preferred imaging modality as it avoids ionising radiation in a child who may carry the RB1 germline mutation predisposing to radiation-induced second malignancies. MRI also assesses for trilateral retinoblastoma. (Source: COSA Retinoblastoma Guidelines 2023)

options.text: Reassure and repeat the red reflex test at the 12-month health check
options.rationale: Reassurance is inappropriate when leukocoria is detected. An absent red reflex in one eye is a red flag requiring urgent assessment regardless of whether the child appears otherwise well. Retinoblastoma can progress rapidly, and delay of 3 months from a potentially curable intraocular tumour to extraocular disease significantly worsens prognosis and may necessitate enucleation. (Source: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Red Reflex Testing 2024)

options.text: Request CT orbit with contrast as the first-line imaging study
options.rationale: CT is not the preferred first-line imaging for suspected retinoblastoma despite its ability to detect intratumoural calcification. Children with retinoblastoma may carry the RB1 germline mutation, which significantly increases susceptibility to radiation-induced second primary malignancies, particularly sarcomas. MRI is the preferred imaging modality to avoid this risk and also provides superior soft tissue detail. (Source: COSA Retinoblastoma Guidelines 2023)

explanation.summary: Leukocoria with an absent red reflex in an infant is a red flag for retinoblastoma and requires urgent ophthalmological assessment. Examination under anaesthesia is essential for detailed fundoscopy and tumour characterisation. MRI is preferred over CT as the first-line imaging study because children with retinoblastoma may carry the RB1 germline mutation, which confers increased susceptibility to radiation-induced second malignancies. Both eyes must be assessed as bilateral disease occurs in approximately 30-40% of cases.
key_points: Leukocoria with absent red reflex requires urgent ophthalmology assessment within 1-2 days
Examination under anaesthesia is essential for complete fundoscopic assessment
MRI is preferred over CT to avoid radiation in potentially RB1-mutation-positive children
Bilateral retinoblastoma occurs in 30-40% of cases and must be excluded
explanation.pearls: CT should be avoided in suspected retinoblastoma because children with the RB1 germline mutation have a 50-fold increased risk of radiation-induced second malignancies. Trilateral retinoblastoma (bilateral retinoblastoma with a pinealoblastoma) occurs in 5-15% of hereditary cases and is detected on brain MRI. The mean age at diagnosis of unilateral retinoblastoma is 24 months, and bilateral disease presents earlier at a mean of 12 months.

subtopic_detail: Posterior fossa tumour presenting with raised intracranial pressure
stem: A 6-year-old boy presents with a 3-week history of progressively worsening early morning headaches associated with vomiting. Over the past 5 days he has developed an unsteady gait and his parents report he has been bumping into objects. He has no significant past medical history. His immunisations are up to date. There is no family history of neurological conditions.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 68 bpm, BP 130/85 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99% on room air, Temp 36.7 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Bilateral papilloedema on fundoscopy, bilateral sixth nerve palsies, truncal ataxia with broad-based gait, past-pointing on finger-nose testing, no limb weakness, no skin lesions
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain with gadolinium: 4 cm heterogeneously enhancing midline posterior fossa mass arising from the fourth ventricle with associated obstructive hydrocephalus, no evidence of leptomeningeal dissemination on cranio-spinal imaging

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence IV dexamethasone and arrange serial lumbar punctures
options.rationale: Lumbar puncture is absolutely contraindicated in the setting of obstructive hydrocephalus and raised intracranial pressure due to a posterior fossa mass. Removal of CSF from below an obstructive lesion can cause transtentorial or tonsillar herniation, which is rapidly fatal. While dexamethasone may reduce perilesional oedema, it does not address the obstructive hydrocephalus. (Source: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Brain Tumours 2024)

options.text: Arrange stereotactic biopsy of the lesion as the initial step
options.rationale: Stereotactic biopsy alone is not the preferred initial approach for a posterior fossa tumour causing obstructive hydrocephalus. The priority is CSF diversion to manage the obstructive hydrocephalus, followed by definitive tumour resection. Stereotactic biopsy provides tissue diagnosis but does not relieve the obstruction or achieve cytoreduction, which are critical for management and prognosis. (Source: COSA Paediatric Brain Tumour Guidelines 2023)

options.text: Start empiric combination chemotherapy before pursuing surgery
options.rationale: Empiric chemotherapy without tissue diagnosis is not appropriate for posterior fossa tumours. The differential diagnosis includes medulloblastoma, ependymoma, and astrocytoma, each requiring different treatment protocols. Furthermore, chemotherapy does not acutely relieve obstructive hydrocephalus, which is the most immediate life-threatening complication requiring urgent neurosurgical intervention. (Source: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Brain Tumours 2024)

options.text: Arrange urgent neurosurgical referral for CSF diversion and resection
options.rationale: A posterior fossa tumour with obstructive hydrocephalus requires urgent neurosurgical management. The immediate priorities are CSF diversion (via external ventricular drain or endoscopic third ventriculostomy) to relieve the obstructive hydrocephalus, followed by maximal safe tumour resection. Tissue diagnosis obtained at surgery guides subsequent adjuvant therapy. IV dexamethasone should be commenced concurrently to reduce perilesional oedema. (Source: COSA Paediatric Brain Tumour Guidelines 2023)

options.text: Commence IV mannitol infusion and arrange transfer to rehabilitation
options.rationale: While IV mannitol can temporarily reduce intracranial pressure as a bridging measure, it does not treat the underlying obstructive hydrocephalus caused by the tumour. Transfer to rehabilitation is premature and inappropriate as the patient requires urgent definitive neurosurgical intervention. Mannitol may be used as a temporising measure during transfer to a neurosurgical centre but is not definitive management. (Source: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Brain Tumours 2024)

explanation.summary: A midline posterior fossa mass with obstructive hydrocephalus in a child is a neurosurgical emergency. The clinical features of morning headaches, vomiting, and cerebellar signs (ataxia, past-pointing) with papilloedema and Cushing response (bradycardia, hypertension) indicate raised intracranial pressure. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated due to herniation risk. The management priorities are CSF diversion to relieve hydrocephalus followed by maximal safe tumour resection to obtain tissue diagnosis and guide adjuvant therapy.
key_points: Lumbar puncture is contraindicated in obstructive hydrocephalus due to herniation risk
CSF diversion is the immediate priority followed by tumour resection
Tissue diagnosis guides subsequent chemotherapy and radiotherapy protocols
The Cushing triad of bradycardia, hypertension, and irregular respirations indicates raised ICP
explanation.pearls: Medulloblastoma is the most common malignant posterior fossa tumour in children (30-40% of posterior fossa tumours), arising from the cerebellar vermis and extending into the fourth ventricle. Extent of surgical resection is the strongest prognostic factor. CSF cytology obtained at surgery (not via lumbar puncture preoperatively) is essential for staging. Postoperative posterior fossa syndrome (cerebellar mutism) occurs in 25% of cases and typically presents 1-2 days after surgery with mutism, emotional lability, and hypotonia.

subtopic_detail: Paediatric sepsis - fluid resuscitation and reassessment
stem: A 2-year-old girl (weight 12 kg) is brought to the emergency department by ambulance with a 12-hour history of fever, poor oral intake, and increasing lethargy. She had an upper respiratory tract infection 3 days ago. On arrival, she is drowsy but rousable, with mottled peripheries. Intravenous access has been established and she has received one 20 mL/kg (240 mL) bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride over 10 minutes. Broad-spectrum antibiotics have been administered. Following the first bolus, she remains tachycardic and hypotensive with mottled skin.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 175 bpm, BP 60/35 mmHg (below 5th centile for age), RR 42, SpO2 94% on high-flow oxygen, Temp 39.8 degrees C, CRT 5 seconds centrally
data_table.Examination: Drowsy but rousable, mottled peripheries, cool extremities, no rash, no meningism, tachypnoeic with no added sounds, abdomen soft, fontanelle closed
data_table.Investigations: WCC 22.5 x10^9/L, neutrophils 18.2 x10^9/L, Hb 112 g/L, platelets 145 x10^9/L, CRP 185 mg/L, lactate 5.8 mmol/L, glucose 6.2 mmol/L, Na 136 mmol/L, K 4.8 mmol/L, creatinine 45 micromol/L, venous pH 7.22, HCO3 14 mmol/L, BE -12

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Switch to 4% albumin solution for the second fluid bolus dose
options.rationale: While albumin is an acceptable resuscitation fluid in paediatric sepsis, switching from normal saline to albumin for the second bolus is not the priority and is not standard Australian practice for initial fluid resuscitation. The FEAST trial demonstrated potential harm with albumin boluses in febrile children in resource-limited settings, though this is debated in high-resource settings. The priority is delivering a second crystalloid bolus and escalating to inotropes. (Source: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Sepsis 2024)

options.text: Commence a dopamine infusion via the peripheral intravenous line
options.rationale: While inotropic support may be needed, dopamine should not be commenced before a second fluid bolus has been given. Australian paediatric sepsis guidelines recommend up to 40-60 mL/kg of isotonic crystalloid in 20 mL/kg aliquots within the first hour before escalating to inotropes. Additionally, adrenaline or noradrenaline are preferred first-line vasopressors over dopamine in paediatric septic shock. (Source: PREDICT Sepsis Guideline 2024)

options.text: Administer a rapid 40 mL/kg crystalloid bolus over 5 minutes now
options.rationale: While further fluid resuscitation is needed, administering 40 mL/kg as a single rapid bolus is excessive and risks fluid overload and pulmonary oedema. Australian guidelines recommend fluid resuscitation in 20 mL/kg aliquots with reassessment after each bolus to guide further fluid administration. Reassessment between boluses allows titration based on clinical response and detection of fluid overload. (Source: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Sepsis 2024)

options.text: Reduce fluid rate to maintenance and commence standard IV fluids
options.rationale: Reducing fluid administration to maintenance rates in a child with fluid-refractory septic shock and ongoing hypotension is inappropriate and potentially fatal. This child has persistent signs of shock (tachycardia, hypotension, mottled skin, prolonged CRT, metabolic acidosis) requiring ongoing aggressive fluid resuscitation and likely escalation to inotropic support if unresponsive to further fluids. (Source: PREDICT Sepsis Guideline 2024)

options.text: Give a second 20 mL/kg bolus and prepare inotropes if no response
options.rationale: This child has septic shock with inadequate response to the first 20 mL/kg fluid bolus. Australian paediatric sepsis guidelines recommend repeated 20 mL/kg isotonic crystalloid boluses (up to 40-60 mL/kg in the first hour) with reassessment after each bolus. If there is no improvement after 40 mL/kg, this is defined as fluid-refractory shock and inotropic support (adrenaline as first-line agent) should be commenced. Central venous access and intra-arterial monitoring should be established. (Source: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Sepsis 2024)

explanation.summary: Paediatric septic shock requires aggressive fluid resuscitation with isotonic crystalloid in 20 mL/kg boluses, reassessing after each bolus. Up to 40-60 mL/kg may be administered in the first hour. If shock persists after 40 mL/kg (fluid-refractory shock), inotropic support with adrenaline as the first-line agent should be commenced. This child has received one 20 mL/kg bolus with persistent hypotension and signs of shock, requiring a second bolus with concurrent preparation for inotrope administration.
key_points: Isotonic crystalloid is administered in 20 mL/kg boluses with reassessment after each bolus
Up to 40-60 mL/kg may be given in the first hour of paediatric sepsis resuscitation
Fluid-refractory shock is defined as persistent shock after 40 mL/kg of crystalloid
Adrenaline is the first-line inotrope for fluid-refractory paediatric septic shock
explanation.pearls: In paediatric septic shock, adrenaline (0.05-0.3 mcg/kg/min) is the first-line inotrope for fluid-refractory cold shock (poor perfusion, cool extremities), while noradrenaline (0.05-0.3 mcg/kg/min) is preferred for warm shock (bounding pulses, flash capillary refill). Hypotension in children is a late sign of shock, and the systolic BP threshold for hypotension in a 2-year-old is approximately 70 mmHg (70 + 2 x age). This child's systolic BP of 60 mmHg confirms decompensated shock.

subtopic_detail: Anorexia nervosa - medical admission criteria
stem: A 15-year-old girl is referred by her GP with a 6-month history of progressively restrictive eating and excessive exercise. Her parents report she has lost approximately 25% of her pre-morbid weight. She denies any problems and is reluctant to engage. She has not had a menstrual period for 4 months.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 42 bpm (overnight), BP 88/52 mmHg supine with postural drop 25 mmHg on standing, T 35.2 C, RR 14
data_table.Examination: BMI 15.2 kg/m2, lanugo hair on arms and back, dry skin with peripheral cyanosis, muscle wasting, parotid enlargement absent
data_table.Investigations: ECG: sinus bradycardia, QTc 480 ms. Na 136 mmol/L, K 3.2 mmol/L, phosphate 0.72 mmol/L, glucose 3.4 mmol/L, Hb 118 g/L, WCC 3.2 x10^9/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Admit for medical stabilisation with cardiac monitoring and supervised refeeding
options.rationale: This patient meets multiple criteria for urgent medical admission: heart rate below 50, postural drop greater than 20 mmHg, hypothermia below 35.5 C, prolonged QTc above 450 ms, hypokalaemia, hypophosphataemia, and weight loss exceeding 20%. Australian guidelines recommend inpatient stabilisation with continuous cardiac monitoring and carefully supervised refeeding to prevent refeeding syndrome, given her high risk profile with low phosphate and potassium. (Source: RCH CPG - Eating Disorders)

options.text: Refer to outpatient dietitian for weekly weigh-ins and structured meal planning
options.rationale: Outpatient management is inappropriate when medical instability criteria are met. This patient has bradycardia, significant postural hypotension, hypothermia, prolonged QTc, and electrolyte derangements that all mandate inpatient care. Outpatient management risks fatal cardiac arrhythmia or refeeding syndrome without monitoring. (Source: RCH CPG - Eating Disorders)

options.text: Commence fluoxetine for comorbid depression and arrange review in two weeks
options.rationale: Fluoxetine is not indicated as a primary treatment for anorexia nervosa and does not improve weight restoration. Additionally, starting an SSRI in a patient with prolonged QTc and electrolyte disturbance risks further QT prolongation and arrhythmia. Medical stabilisation must precede any psychiatric pharmacotherapy. (Source: eTG - Eating Disorders)

options.text: Enrol in an eating disorder day program with family-based therapy sessions
options.rationale: Day programs and family-based therapy are important components of eating disorder treatment but are appropriate for medically stable patients. This patient's cardiovascular instability, hypothermia, and electrolyte abnormalities require inpatient medical stabilisation before transitioning to a day program model. (Source: RCH CPG - Eating Disorders)

options.text: Arrange psychiatric admission for compulsory treatment under the Mental Health Act
options.rationale: While involuntary treatment may be considered in eating disorders when a patient refuses life-saving care, the immediate priority is medical stabilisation on a medical ward with cardiac monitoring. Psychiatric admission alone does not provide the level of medical monitoring required for her cardiovascular and metabolic instability. (Source: RANZCP - Eating Disorders Guidelines)

explanation.summary: This patient with anorexia nervosa meets multiple criteria for urgent medical admission including bradycardia below 50 bpm, postural drop exceeding 20 mmHg, hypothermia, prolonged QTc, and electrolyte derangement. Australian guidelines mandate inpatient medical stabilisation with continuous cardiac monitoring and carefully supervised refeeding. Refeeding syndrome is a major risk given her low phosphate and potassium levels, requiring gradual caloric increase with phosphate and electrolyte monitoring.
key_points: Medical admission criteria in anorexia include HR below 50, postural drop above 20 mmHg, temperature below 35.5 C, QTc above 450 ms, and weight loss above 20%
Refeeding syndrome risk is highest when phosphate is low at baseline and caloric restriction has been prolonged
QTc prolongation above 450 ms confers risk of torsades de pointes particularly with concurrent hypokalaemia
explanation.pearls: Refeeding syndrome typically develops within the first 72 hours of nutritional rehabilitation. Phosphate should be supplemented prophylactically and monitored 6-hourly in the first 3 days. Initial caloric prescription should start at approximately 1200-1400 kcal/day and increase by 200-300 kcal every 2-3 days with electrolyte surveillance. A QTc above 500 ms is a medical emergency requiring magnesium correction and continuous telemetry.

subtopic_detail: ADHD medication initiation in adolescent - first-line choice
stem: A 14-year-old boy is reviewed in clinic following a comprehensive ADHD assessment. Conners parent and teacher rating scales are consistent with ADHD-combined type. School reports document declining academic performance and behavioural difficulties. He has completed a structured behavioural management programme over 6 months with limited effect on symptoms. He has no cardiac history, no family history of sudden death, and no history of substance use.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78 bpm, BP 118/72 mmHg, weight 55 kg, height 168 cm
data_table.Examination: Normal cardiovascular examination, no murmurs, normal neurological examination
data_table.Investigations: ECG: normal sinus rhythm, QTc 410 ms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate pharmacological management?

options.text: Commence atomoxetine 40 mg daily as the preferred first-line agent
options.rationale: Atomoxetine is a non-stimulant medication reserved as second-line treatment in Australia when stimulant medications are contraindicated, poorly tolerated, or ineffective. It has a slower onset of action taking 4-6 weeks to reach full effect and is generally less effective than stimulant medications for ADHD symptom control. (Source: AMH - ADHD)

options.text: Commence methylphenidate immediate-release at low dose with titration
