options.rationale: Methylphenidate is the recommended first-line pharmacological treatment for ADHD in Australian guidelines when behavioural strategies have been insufficient. It should be started at a low dose of 5 mg once or twice daily and titrated based on symptom response and side effects. Australian PBS subsidises methylphenidate as first-line and requires baseline cardiovascular assessment including ECG before initiation. (Source: AMH - ADHD)

options.text: Commence dexamfetamine extended-release capsules as initial treatment
options.rationale: While dexamfetamine is an effective stimulant for ADHD, Australian guidelines recommend methylphenidate as the first-line stimulant in most states. Dexamfetamine is typically considered when methylphenidate is ineffective or not tolerated. Extended-release formulations may be considered later but initial titration with immediate-release allows better dose finding. (Source: eTG - ADHD)

options.text: Trial omega-3 fatty acid supplementation before considering stimulants
options.rationale: Omega-3 fatty acid supplementation has limited evidence for ADHD symptom improvement and is not recommended as a standalone treatment. This patient has already completed behavioural strategies without adequate benefit and meets criteria for pharmacological treatment. Delaying evidence-based medication is not appropriate at this stage. (Source: NHMRC - ADHD Guidelines)

options.text: Refer for cognitive behavioural therapy as sole ongoing management
options.rationale: CBT alone is not first-line management for moderate-to-severe ADHD in adolescents. While psychological therapies are useful as adjunctive treatments, pharmacological management with a stimulant medication is indicated when behavioural strategies have been trialled and found insufficient, as in this case with documented academic decline. (Source: eTG - ADHD)

explanation.summary: Methylphenidate is the recommended first-line pharmacological treatment for ADHD in Australian guidelines when behavioural interventions have been insufficient. It should be commenced at a low dose and titrated upward based on symptom response. A baseline ECG and cardiovascular assessment are required before initiation. Extended-release formulations can be considered once an effective dose is established with immediate-release preparations.
key_points: Methylphenidate immediate-release is first-line pharmacotherapy for ADHD in Australia following inadequate response to behavioural strategies
Starting dose is 5 mg once or twice daily with gradual titration over 4-6 weeks
Baseline ECG, heart rate, blood pressure, height, and weight are required before commencing stimulant medication
explanation.pearls: Methylphenidate should be started at 5 mg daily or twice daily and increased by 5 mg increments weekly to a maximum of 60 mg/day in divided doses. Blood pressure and heart rate should be monitored at each dose adjustment. Growth should be plotted on centile charts 3-6 monthly as stimulants can suppress appetite and linear growth. A drug holiday during school breaks may be considered to assess ongoing need.

subtopic_detail: Eczema herpeticum recognition and management
stem: A 3-year-old girl with moderate atopic eczema managed with regular emollients and mometasone furoate 0.1% ointment is brought to the emergency department. Over 2 days she has developed widespread clustered vesicles and punched-out erosions over her face, neck, and upper chest. She is febrile, irritable, and refusing to eat. There is no history of contact with anyone with cold sores, though she attends day care.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 145 bpm, T 39.2 C, RR 28, BP 90/55 mmHg, SpO2 98% on room air
data_table.Examination: Clustered monomorphic vesicles and shallow punched-out erosions on face, neck, and upper chest, some with haemorrhagic crusting, periorbital oedema bilaterally, no corneal involvement on fluorescein examination
data_table.Investigations: Tzanck smear: multinucleated giant cells identified. FBC: WCC 14.2 x10^9/L, Hb 112 g/L, platelets 280 x10^9/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Increase topical corticosteroid potency and add emollient wraps
options.rationale: Increasing topical corticosteroid potency would worsen this condition as corticosteroids promote local immunosuppression and facilitate viral spread. Topical corticosteroids should be withheld in eczema herpeticum until the viral infection is controlled, as ongoing use can lead to dissemination and systemic illness. (Source: RCH CPG - Eczema Herpeticum)

options.text: Commence oral flucloxacillin for secondary bacterial skin infection
options.rationale: The clinical presentation of monomorphic punched-out erosions with positive Tzanck smear indicates a viral rather than bacterial aetiology. While secondary bacterial infection may coexist, treating only for bacterial infection without antiviral therapy would allow ongoing HSV dissemination and risks systemic complications. (Source: eTG - Dermatology)

options.text: Commence IV aciclovir and withhold topical corticosteroids
options.rationale: Eczema herpeticum is a dermatological emergency requiring prompt IV aciclovir in unwell children or those with extensive disease. The dose is 10-20 mg/kg 8-hourly depending on age. Topical corticosteroids must be withheld as they promote viral replication and dissemination. Australian guidelines recommend IV over oral treatment when the child is febrile and has widespread involvement. (Source: RCH CPG - Eczema Herpeticum)

options.text: Apply topical mupirocin ointment to erosions three times daily
options.rationale: Topical mupirocin is an antibacterial agent that would not treat the underlying HSV infection causing eczema herpeticum. Mupirocin may be considered as adjunctive treatment for secondary impetiginisation but must not replace systemic antiviral therapy which is the definitive treatment for this condition. (Source: eTG - Dermatology)

options.text: Commence oral valaciclovir and continue topical corticosteroids
options.rationale: While valaciclovir provides antiviral coverage, oral therapy is reserved for mild localised disease in a well child. This patient is febrile with extensive facial and truncal involvement requiring IV aciclovir. Additionally, continuing topical corticosteroids is contraindicated as it promotes viral spread and delays healing. (Source: RCH CPG - Eczema Herpeticum)

explanation.summary: Eczema herpeticum is a medical emergency caused by disseminated HSV infection in patients with pre-existing atopic eczema. It presents with monomorphic punched-out erosions and grouped vesicles, often with systemic features. IV aciclovir is required for unwell children or those with extensive disease, and topical corticosteroids must be withheld as they exacerbate viral dissemination. Ophthalmology review is essential when periorbital involvement is present.
key_points: Eczema herpeticum requires IV aciclovir 10-20 mg/kg 8-hourly in febrile or extensively affected children
Topical corticosteroids must be withheld until viral infection is controlled
Tzanck smear showing multinucleated giant cells supports HSV diagnosis while PCR swab confirms
explanation.pearls: IV aciclovir for eczema herpeticum in children is dosed at 500 mg/m2 or 10-20 mg/kg 8-hourly for a minimum of 5 days, transitioning to oral once afebrile and no new lesions for 24-48 hours. Adequate IV hydration is essential to prevent aciclovir-induced crystalline nephropathy. If periorbital involvement is present, urgent ophthalmology review is required to exclude herpetic keratitis, which can cause corneal scarring.

subtopic_detail: Immune thrombocytopenia - management based on bleeding and count
stem: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents who noticed widespread bruising and small red spots on his legs and trunk over the past week. He has been otherwise well with no recent fevers, weight loss, bone pain, or lymphadenopathy. He had a viral upper respiratory tract infection 3 weeks ago. He is active and playful in the department.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 100 bpm, T 36.8 C, BP 95/60 mmHg, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Widespread petechiae on lower limbs and trunk, scattered bruising on shins and arms, no mucosal bleeding, no hepatosplenomegaly, no lymphadenopathy, well-appearing child
data_table.Investigations: Platelet count 8 x10^9/L, Hb 128 g/L, WCC 7.4 x10^9/L, neutrophils 3.8 x10^9/L, blood film: isolated thrombocytopenia with large platelets, no blasts

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day for a four-day course
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are recommended in ITP when there is clinically significant mucosal bleeding such as epistaxis, oral blood blisters, or haematuria. In the absence of mucosal bleeding, observation is preferred regardless of platelet count as the bleeding risk correlates more with clinical bleeding severity than absolute platelet number. (Source: RCH CPG - Immune Thrombocytopenia)

options.text: Administer intravenous immunoglobulin 1 g/kg as a single infusion
options.rationale: IV immunoglobulin is reserved for children with ITP who have significant mucosal or more serious bleeding, or who require rapid platelet rise before an urgent procedure. It is not indicated for skin-only bleeding even with very low platelet counts. Side effects include headache, fever, and risk of aseptic meningitis. (Source: RCH CPG - Immune Thrombocytopenia)

options.text: Arrange urgent bone marrow aspirate to exclude acute leukaemia
options.rationale: Bone marrow aspirate is not routinely required in typical ITP presenting with isolated thrombocytopenia in an otherwise well child. The blood film confirms isolated thrombocytopenia with large platelets and no blasts. Bone marrow should be considered if there are atypical features such as abnormalities in other cell lines or hepatosplenomegaly. (Source: eTG - Haematology)

options.text: Observe with restricted activity and outpatient follow-up within 48 hours
options.rationale: In a well child with newly diagnosed ITP and only cutaneous bleeding without mucosal haemorrhage, observation with activity restriction is the recommended approach regardless of platelet count. Australian guidelines emphasise that treatment decisions should be based on bleeding severity rather than platelet number alone. Close follow-up within 24-48 hours ensures monitoring for any progression to mucosal bleeding. (Source: RCH CPG - Immune Thrombocytopenia)

options.text: Commence tranexamic acid and arrange urgent platelet transfusion
options.rationale: Platelet transfusion is not indicated in ITP as transfused platelets are rapidly consumed by the same autoimmune process. Platelet transfusion is reserved for life-threatening haemorrhage in combination with other therapies. Tranexamic acid may be considered as adjunctive therapy for mucosal bleeding but is not indicated for cutaneous-only manifestations. (Source: RCH CPG - Immune Thrombocytopenia)

explanation.summary: Newly diagnosed immune thrombocytopenia in children is managed based on bleeding severity rather than platelet count alone. In a well child with cutaneous-only bleeding and no mucosal haemorrhage, observation with activity restriction and close follow-up is appropriate even when the platelet count is very low. Treatment with corticosteroids or IV immunoglobulin is reserved for clinically significant mucosal bleeding or more severe haemorrhagic manifestations.
key_points: ITP management is guided by clinical bleeding severity rather than absolute platelet count
Cutaneous-only bleeding in a well child warrants observation with activity restriction and close follow-up
Corticosteroids and IVIG are reserved for mucosal bleeding or more significant haemorrhage
explanation.pearls: Approximately 80% of childhood ITP resolves spontaneously within 6 months. Activity restriction involves avoiding contact sports and activities with high risk of head injury while platelets are below 50 x10^9/L. Parents should be counselled to return immediately for mucosal bleeding, headache, or altered consciousness. If prednisolone is needed, a short course of 4 mg/kg/day for 4 days is preferred over prolonged tapering courses.

subtopic_detail: Major paediatric burns - fluid resuscitation calculation
stem: A 7-year-old boy (25 kg) is brought to the emergency department after pulling a pot of boiling water off the stove 1 hour ago. He has sustained scald burns to his anterior torso and both upper limbs. Using the Lund and Browder chart, the total body surface area burned is estimated at 25%. The burns are mixed superficial and deep partial thickness. He is alert, crying, and in significant pain. IV access has been obtained in an unburned limb.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 130 bpm, BP 100/65 mmHg, T 36.0 C, RR 24, SpO2 99% on room air, CRT 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Erythematous and blistered areas across anterior chest, abdomen, and bilateral upper limbs, skin moist with intact sensation, no circumferential burns, airway patent with no soot or facial burns
data_table.Investigations: Weight 25 kg, urine output not yet measured, Hb 135 g/L, Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, glucose 7.2 mmol/L, lactate 2.8 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate fluid resuscitation strategy?

options.text: Administer 20 mL/kg normal saline bolus then commence standard maintenance
options.rationale: A standard fluid bolus approach is appropriate for hypovolaemic shock from other causes but does not account for the ongoing massive capillary leak in major burns. Burns exceeding 10% TBSA in children require formula-based resuscitation that accounts for the degree and extent of burn injury to guide replacement over 24 hours. (Source: RCH CPG - Burns)

options.text: Commence colloid resuscitation with 4% albumin infusion over 4 hours
options.rationale: Colloid resuscitation is not recommended in the initial 8-24 hours of burns management as increased capillary permeability causes colloid to leak into the interstitium, worsening oedema without effective intravascular expansion. Crystalloid is the standard resuscitation fluid for the first 24 hours following major burns injury. (Source: ANZBA - Burns Guidelines)

options.text: Apply silver sulfadiazine dressings and encourage oral fluid intake only
options.rationale: Oral fluid intake alone is insufficient for burns exceeding 10% TBSA in children. This child has 25% TBSA burns requiring IV fluid resuscitation. Additionally, silver sulfadiazine is no longer first-line for burns dressings in many Australian centres due to delayed wound healing, and definitive dressing is secondary to establishing resuscitation. (Source: RCH CPG - Burns)

options.text: Commence half-normal saline at double the standard maintenance fluid rate
options.rationale: Maintenance-based calculations significantly underestimate fluid requirements in major burns. A 25% TBSA burn causes massive third-space losses that require formula-based resuscitation. Half-normal saline is also not the appropriate crystalloid for burns resuscitation, as isotonic solutions such as Hartmann solution are preferred to avoid hyponatraemia. (Source: ANZBA - Burns Guidelines)

options.text: Commence Parkland formula fluids with urinary catheter output monitoring
options.rationale: The modified Parkland formula using 3 mL/kg/% TBSA of Hartmann solution is standard for paediatric burns resuscitation in Australia. For this child: 3 x 25 x 25 = 1875 mL over 24 hours, with half given in the first 8 hours from time of injury. A urinary catheter is essential to titrate fluids to urine output of 1 mL/kg/hour in children. Maintenance fluids with dextrose are also added in young children. (Source: RCH CPG - Burns)

explanation.summary: Major paediatric burns exceeding 10% TBSA require formal fluid resuscitation using the modified Parkland formula at 3 mL/kg/% TBSA of crystalloid over 24 hours. Half of the calculated volume is given in the first 8 hours from the time of injury, not from the time of presentation. Urinary catheter insertion is essential to titrate fluids to a target urine output of 1 mL/kg/hour. Children under 30 kg also require maintenance fluids containing dextrose to prevent hypoglycaemia.
key_points: Modified Parkland formula: 3 mL/kg/% TBSA of Hartmann solution over 24 hours, half in the first 8 hours from injury time
Target urine output for paediatric burns resuscitation is 1 mL/kg/hour
Children under 30 kg require maintenance fluids with dextrose in addition to burns resuscitation fluids
explanation.pearls: For this 25 kg child with 25% TBSA burns, the Parkland calculation is 3 x 25 x 25 = 1875 mL over 24 hours. Since 1 hour has elapsed, approximately 937 mL should be given over the next 7 hours (134 mL/hour), then the remaining 937 mL over the next 16 hours (59 mL/hour). Maintenance fluid with 5% dextrose must be added at 65 mL/hour. An initial bolus of 10-20 mL/kg may be given if there are signs of shock but does not replace the formula calculation.

subtopic_detail: PPROM at 32 weeks - corticosteroid timing and antibiotics
stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 presents at 32+2 weeks gestation with a history of a sudden gush of clear fluid 3 hours ago. She denies contractions, vaginal bleeding, or fever. Her previous pregnancy was an uncomplicated term vaginal delivery. A sterile speculum examination reveals pooling of clear fluid in the posterior vaginal fornix. An AmniSure test is positive. Her GBS status from routine screening is not yet available.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 82 bpm, BP 118/74 mmHg, T 36.9 C, RR 16
data_table.Examination: Uterus soft, non-tender, cephalic presentation, no contractions palpable, cervix not digitally examined
data_table.Investigations: CTG: baseline 140 bpm, moderate variability, accelerations present, no decelerations. WCC 10.2 x10^9/L, CRP 4 mg/L, urine MCS sent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Administer betamethasone 11.4 mg IM and commence erythromycin for 10 days
options.rationale: PPROM between 24 and 34 weeks requires antenatal corticosteroids for foetal lung maturation and prophylactic antibiotics to prolong latency and reduce infectious morbidity. Australian RANZCOG guidelines recommend betamethasone 11.4 mg IM as a single dose repeated at 24 hours, and erythromycin 250 mg 6-hourly for 10 days as the preferred antibiotic regimen for PPROM based on the ORACLE trial evidence. (Source: RANZCOG - PPROM Guidelines)

options.text: Plan immediate delivery by caesarean section given preterm rupture status
options.rationale: Immediate delivery is not indicated at 32 weeks in the absence of chorioamnionitis, foetal distress, or cord prolapse. Expectant management with corticosteroids and antibiotics is the standard approach for PPROM between 24 and 34 weeks, aiming to balance the benefits of in-utero foetal maturation against the risks of ascending infection. (Source: RANZCOG - PPROM Guidelines)

options.text: Commence IV ampicillin and plan induction of labour within 24 hours
options.rationale: IV ampicillin and immediate delivery are not appropriate at 32 weeks without signs of infection. Expectant management is preferred at this gestation. The ORACLE trial demonstrated that erythromycin is the preferred antibiotic for PPROM as co-amoxiclav was associated with increased risk of neonatal necrotising enterocolitis. (Source: eTG - Obstetrics)

options.text: Administer dexamethasone and plan elective delivery at 34 weeks gestation
options.rationale: While corticosteroids are correct, betamethasone is preferred over dexamethasone in Australian practice for antenatal lung maturation as it has a more favourable safety profile. Planning delivery at a fixed 34 weeks is overly prescriptive; delivery timing depends on clinical course, and antibiotic prophylaxis is also required. (Source: RANZCOG - PPROM Guidelines)

options.text: Observe without corticosteroids as foetal lungs are likely mature enough
options.rationale: At 32 weeks gestation, foetal lung maturity cannot be assumed. Antenatal corticosteroids provide significant benefit in reducing respiratory distress syndrome, intraventricular haemorrhage, and neonatal mortality when given between 24 and 34 weeks. Withholding corticosteroids at this gestation is not consistent with guideline recommendations. (Source: RANZCOG - PPROM Guidelines)

explanation.summary: PPROM at 32 weeks requires a combined approach of antenatal corticosteroids for foetal lung maturation and prophylactic antibiotics to prolong latency. Betamethasone 11.4 mg IM is given as two doses 24 hours apart, and erythromycin 250 mg 6-hourly for 10 days is the recommended antibiotic based on the ORACLE trial. Expectant management is appropriate in the absence of chorioamnionitis, cord prolapse, or non-reassuring foetal status.
key_points: Betamethasone 11.4 mg IM x 2 doses (24 hours apart) is the preferred corticosteroid for antenatal lung maturation between 24 and 34 weeks
Erythromycin 250 mg 6-hourly for 10 days is the recommended antibiotic regimen in PPROM
Co-amoxiclav is avoided in PPROM due to increased risk of neonatal necrotising enterocolitis
explanation.pearls: The ORACLE trial demonstrated that erythromycin in PPROM prolongs pregnancy by a median of 5 days and reduces neonatal morbidity. Co-amoxiclav was associated with a significantly higher rate of neonatal NEC and should be avoided. Digital vaginal examination should not be performed as it shortens latency and increases infection risk. If GBS status is unknown, intrapartum GBS prophylaxis with benzylpenicillin should be given when labour commences.

subtopic_detail: Lactational mastitis progressing to abscess - management
stem: A 30-year-old woman presents 4 weeks postpartum with a 5-day history of worsening pain and swelling in her right breast. She was commenced on oral flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily by her GP 3 days ago for mastitis but has not improved. She is continuing to breastfeed from both breasts. She has no drug allergies.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 96 bpm, BP 115/72 mmHg, T 38.8 C, RR 18
data_table.Examination: Right breast erythematous and swollen in upper outer quadrant, fluctuant 4 cm mass palpable, tender, overlying skin tense but not necrotic, left breast normal, no axillary lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: Breast ultrasound: 3.5 cm well-defined hypoechoic collection with internal debris in the upper outer quadrant consistent with an abscess, no other collections identified. WCC 15.8 x10^9/L, CRP 89 mg/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Switch to IV antibiotics and review clinical response at 48 hours
options.rationale: IV antibiotics alone will not adequately treat a formed breast abscess. Once an abscess has walled off, antibiotics cannot penetrate the cavity effectively and drainage is required. Antibiotics are important as adjunctive therapy but the collection must be drained to achieve source control and clinical resolution. (Source: eTG - Breast Infection)

options.text: Perform ultrasound-guided needle aspiration and continue oral antibiotics
options.rationale: Ultrasound-guided needle aspiration is the recommended first-line drainage procedure for lactational breast abscesses less than 5 cm. It is less invasive than surgical drainage, can be performed under local anaesthesia, preserves breastfeeding capability, and has good success rates with repeat aspiration if needed. Oral flucloxacillin should be continued as the most likely organism is Staphylococcus aureus. (Source: RANZCOG - Breast Conditions in Pregnancy and Lactation)
source_refs: RANZCOG - Breast Conditions in Pregnancy and Lactation

options.text: Arrange formal surgical incision and drainage under general anaesthesia
options.rationale: Surgical incision and drainage under general anaesthesia is reserved for large abscesses above 5 cm, multiloculated collections, recurrent abscesses failing aspiration, or when skin necrosis is present. For a 3.5 cm unilocular abscess, ultrasound-guided needle aspiration is preferred as it is less invasive and has lower complication rates. (Source: eTG - Breast Infection)

options.text: Cease breastfeeding from both breasts and commence cabergoline
options.rationale: Cessation of breastfeeding is not recommended in lactational mastitis or breast abscess as continued feeding or expressing helps resolve infection and maintain milk supply. Cabergoline to suppress lactation is not indicated unless the patient wishes to cease breastfeeding. Breastfeeding from the affected side is safe for the infant. (Source: RANZCOG - Breast Conditions in Pregnancy and Lactation)
source_refs: RANZCOG - Breast Conditions in Pregnancy and Lactation

options.text: Increase flucloxacillin dose to 1 g four times daily and add metronidazole
options.rationale: Increasing antibiotic dose and adding metronidazole will not resolve a formed abscess without drainage. Anaerobic coverage with metronidazole is not routinely required for lactational breast abscesses as the predominant organism is Staphylococcus aureus. The collection must be drained for clinical improvement. (Source: eTG - Breast Infection)

explanation.summary: Lactational breast abscess confirmed on ultrasound requires drainage in addition to continued antibiotics. Ultrasound-guided needle aspiration is the recommended first-line procedure for abscesses less than 5 cm as it is less invasive, can be performed under local anaesthesia, and preserves breastfeeding capacity. Breastfeeding should be continued from both breasts as it aids resolution and is safe for the infant.
key_points: Ultrasound-guided needle aspiration is first-line for lactational breast abscesses less than 5 cm
Antibiotics alone cannot resolve a formed abscess and drainage is essential
Breastfeeding should continue from both breasts during treatment of breast abscess
explanation.pearls: Aspiration may need to be repeated 2-3 times over 7-10 days in up to 50% of cases. If the abscess recurs after 3 aspirations, surgical drainage should be considered. Aspirated fluid should be sent for microscopy, culture, and sensitivity to guide antibiotic therapy. If MRSA is suspected based on local prevalence, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole can be added. The aspiration needle should ideally enter through healthy skin rather than the most fluctuant point to reduce risk of fistula formation.

sources.label: RANZCOG - Breast Conditions in Pregnancy and Lactation

subtopic_detail: Pelvic organ prolapse - management selection based on stage and symptoms
stem: A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with a 12-month history of worsening vaginal heaviness and a sensation of something protruding from the vagina. She reports urinary frequency, difficulty emptying her bladder, and needing to digitally reduce the prolapse to void. She is not sexually active and has no stress incontinence. She completed supervised pelvic floor muscle training for 6 months with a physiotherapist with minimal improvement. Her BMI is 28 kg/m2.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 72 bpm, BP 134/82 mmHg
data_table.Examination: POP-Q examination: stage II anterior vaginal wall prolapse extending to the hymen on Valsalva, mild posterior wall laxity, no uterine descent, atrophic vaginal mucosa, post-void residual volume 120 mL on bladder scan
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis: no haematuria, no infection. Renal function: normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Proceed to anterior colporrhaphy as definitive surgical correction
options.rationale: Surgical repair should not be the first intervention after failed pelvic floor exercises. Australian guidelines recommend a trial of vaginal pessary before considering surgical options. Anterior colporrhaphy has a significant recurrence rate of 30-40% and carries surgical risks that should be avoided when conservative options have not been fully explored. (Source: RANZCOG - Pelvic Organ Prolapse)

options.text: Prescribe duloxetine 40 mg daily for her urinary voiding symptoms
options.rationale: Duloxetine is a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor that may be used for stress urinary incontinence but has no role in obstructive voiding symptoms caused by pelvic organ prolapse. Her urinary symptoms are due to mechanical obstruction from the cystocele rather than a neurological or sphincter dysfunction. (Source: eTG - Urogynaecology)

options.text: Trial vaginal pessary fitting combined with topical vaginal oestrogen
options.rationale: A vaginal pessary is the recommended next step after failed pelvic floor exercises for symptomatic pelvic organ prolapse. Ring pessaries are commonly used for stage II prolapse and can relieve both the prolapse and associated voiding dysfunction. Topical oestrogen improves vaginal mucosal health, reduces pessary-related erosion, and enhances pessary retention in postmenopausal women with atrophic mucosa. (Source: RANZCOG - Pelvic Organ Prolapse)

options.text: Recommend structured weight loss program and repeat pelvic floor training
options.rationale: While weight reduction is beneficial for prolapse management, this patient has a BMI of 28 which is only mildly overweight, and she has already completed 6 months of supervised pelvic floor training without adequate improvement. Repeating the same failed intervention without adding further management would not adequately address her symptomatic prolapse and voiding dysfunction. (Source: RANZCOG - Pelvic Organ Prolapse)

options.text: Commence systemic hormone replacement therapy for prolapse support
options.rationale: Systemic HRT is not indicated for the treatment of pelvic organ prolapse. Topical vaginal oestrogen is preferred as it provides local mucosal benefit without systemic risks. Systemic HRT does not reverse or significantly improve prolapse and carries cardiovascular and breast cancer risks in this age group without clear benefit for her presenting complaint. (Source: eTG - Urogynaecology)

explanation.summary: For symptomatic stage II pelvic organ prolapse after failed pelvic floor muscle training, a vaginal pessary trial is the recommended next step before considering surgery. Topical vaginal oestrogen should be commenced concurrently in postmenopausal women with vaginal atrophy to improve mucosal health and reduce pessary-related complications. Surgical repair is reserved for women who decline or fail pessary management.
key_points: Vaginal pessary is first-line conservative management after failed pelvic floor training for symptomatic prolapse
Topical oestrogen reduces pessary-related erosion and improves vaginal mucosal health in postmenopausal women
Anterior colporrhaphy has a 30-40% recurrence rate and should be considered only after conservative options
explanation.pearls: Ring pessaries are most suitable for stage I-II prolapse, while Gellhorn or cube pessaries may be needed for more advanced prolapse. Pessaries should be reviewed at 2 weeks after initial fitting, then 3-6 monthly thereafter. Topical oestrogen should be commenced 2 weeks before pessary insertion when possible. If the post-void residual exceeds 200 mL persistently, further urodynamic assessment is warranted.

subtopic_detail: Postpartum endometritis - empiric antibiotic selection
stem: A 26-year-old G1P1 is reviewed on day 3 following an emergency caesarean section for failure to progress in the first stage of labour. She had prolonged rupture of membranes for 22 hours prior to delivery and multiple vaginal examinations during labour. She has developed worsening lower abdominal pain, fever, and malodorous vaginal discharge over the past 12 hours. She is breastfeeding and has no drug allergies.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 105 bpm, BP 108/68 mmHg, T 38.6 C, RR 20
data_table.Examination: Abdomen tender in the suprapubic region, uterine fundus palpable at the umbilicus and markedly tender on bimanual examination, offensive lochia, caesarean wound clean with no erythema or discharge
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18.2 x10^9/L, neutrophils 15.4 x10^9/L, CRP 145 mg/L, Hb 105 g/L, blood cultures taken, high vaginal swab sent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily with outpatient review
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics with outpatient management are insufficient for postpartum endometritis following caesarean section. This patient has significant systemic inflammatory features with high fever, tachycardia, and markedly elevated inflammatory markers that warrant inpatient IV antibiotic therapy. Outpatient management risks progression to sepsis. (Source: eTG - Obstetric Infections)

options.text: IV cefazolin 2 g 8-hourly as single-agent intravenous therapy
options.rationale: Cefazolin monotherapy provides inadequate coverage for postpartum endometritis which is typically a polymicrobial infection involving aerobic gram-positive, gram-negative, and anaerobic organisms. Single-agent first-generation cephalosporin does not cover the anaerobic organisms commonly implicated in endometritis. (Source: RANZCOG - Postpartum Sepsis)

options.text: IV piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g 8-hourly as broad-spectrum cover
options.rationale: Piperacillin-tazobactam provides broad-spectrum coverage but represents an unnecessarily broad empiric choice for uncomplicated postpartum endometritis. Australian antimicrobial stewardship guidelines recommend reserving piperacillin-tazobactam for more serious infections or when narrower-spectrum regimens have failed to promote antibiotic stewardship. (Source: eTG - Obstetric Infections)

options.text: IV ampicillin, gentamicin, and metronidazole as triple combination
options.rationale: The combination of IV ampicillin, gentamicin, and metronidazole is the recommended empiric regimen for postpartum endometritis in Australian guidelines. This triple therapy provides coverage against the polymicrobial organisms involved: ampicillin for streptococci and enterococci, gentamicin for gram-negative bacilli, and metronidazole for anaerobes. All three agents are compatible with breastfeeding. (Source: eTG - Obstetric Infections)

options.text: Oral metronidazole 400 mg 8-hourly with oral erythromycin 500 mg daily
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics are inadequate for established postpartum endometritis with systemic inflammatory signs. This combination also provides incomplete coverage, lacking adequate gram-negative cover. Endometritis following caesarean section with prolonged rupture of membranes requires intravenous therapy with broad polymicrobial coverage. (Source: RANZCOG - Postpartum Sepsis)

explanation.summary: Postpartum endometritis following caesarean section is a polymicrobial infection requiring broad intravenous antibiotic coverage. The recommended Australian empiric regimen is IV ampicillin, gentamicin, and metronidazole, providing coverage against gram-positive, gram-negative, and anaerobic organisms respectively. Risk factors include caesarean delivery, prolonged rupture of membranes, and multiple vaginal examinations. All three antibiotics are compatible with breastfeeding.
key_points: Triple IV therapy with ampicillin, gentamicin, and metronidazole is standard empiric treatment for postpartum endometritis
Endometritis is polymicrobial requiring coverage of gram-positive, gram-negative, and anaerobic organisms
Risk factors include caesarean section, prolonged membrane rupture, and multiple vaginal examinations
explanation.pearls: Gentamicin dosing in postpartum endometritis is typically 4-5 mg/kg IV daily with trough level monitoring. Clinical improvement is expected within 48-72 hours of commencing IV antibiotics. If no improvement occurs, consider retained products, wound infection, pelvic abscess, or septic pelvic thrombophlebitis. Antibiotics can be transitioned to oral therapy 24-48 hours after clinical improvement and should continue for a total course of 5-7 days.

subtopic_detail: PCOS fertility treatment - stepwise approach
stem: A 29-year-old woman presents to a fertility clinic with an 18-month history of inability to conceive. She has a diagnosis of polycystic ovary syndrome based on oligo-ovulation with cycles every 60-90 days, clinical hyperandrogenism with hirsutism, and polycystic ovarian morphology on ultrasound. She has completed 3 months of structured lifestyle modification with dietary counselling and exercise, achieving 5% weight loss but remaining anovulatory. Her partner has a normal semen analysis.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/78 mmHg, HR 76 bpm
data_table.Examination: BMI 32 kg/m2, Ferriman-Gallwey score 12, no acanthosis nigricans, no thyromegaly, normal pelvic examination
data_table.Investigations: Day 2 FSH 5.2 IU/L, LH 14.8 IU/L, oestradiol 180 pmol/L, testosterone 2.8 nmol/L, SHBG 22 nmol/L, TSH 2.1 mIU/L, prolactin 280 mIU/L, fasting glucose 5.1 mmol/L, HbA1c 38 mmol/mol, AMH 48 pmol/L. Hysterosalpingogram: bilateral tubal patency confirmed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in fertility management?

options.text: Commence clomiphene citrate 50 mg daily for days 2 to 6 of cycle
options.rationale: Clomiphene citrate was previously considered first-line for ovulation induction in PCOS but international and Australian guidelines now recommend letrozole as the preferred first-line agent based on superior ovulation, pregnancy, and live birth rates demonstrated in the NEJM 2014 trial by Legro et al. (Source: RANZCOG - PCOS and Infertility)

options.text: Proceed directly to in vitro fertilisation given the prolonged duration
options.rationale: IVF is not first-line management for anovulatory infertility due to PCOS. Ovulation induction with oral agents should be trialled first as it is less invasive, less costly, and effective in the majority of women with PCOS. IVF is reserved for those who fail ovulation induction or have additional infertility factors. (Source: RANZCOG - PCOS and Infertility)

options.text: Add metformin 500 mg three times daily as sole ovulation induction agent
options.rationale: Metformin monotherapy has lower ovulation and pregnancy rates compared to letrozole or clomiphene and is not recommended as a sole ovulation induction agent. While metformin may have a role as adjunctive therapy, particularly in women with insulin resistance, it should not replace first-line ovulation induction agents. (Source: eTG - Infertility)

options.text: Commence low-dose gonadotrophin injections with ultrasound monitoring
options.rationale: Gonadotrophin injections are second-line treatment for ovulation induction in PCOS, reserved for women who fail oral agents. They carry higher risks of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome and multiple pregnancy, require intensive ultrasound monitoring, and are significantly more expensive than oral ovulation induction agents. (Source: RANZCOG - PCOS and Infertility)

options.text: Commence letrozole 2.5 mg daily for days 2 to 6 as ovulation induction
options.rationale: Letrozole is now recommended as first-line ovulation induction for anovulatory infertility in PCOS by international and Australian guidelines. The landmark NEJM trial by Legro et al. demonstrated superior ovulation rates, higher cumulative live birth rates, and lower multiple pregnancy rates compared to clomiphene. The starting dose is 2.5 mg daily on cycle days 2-6 with follicular tracking. (Source: RANZCOG - PCOS and Infertility)

explanation.summary: Letrozole is the recommended first-line ovulation induction agent for anovulatory infertility in PCOS, superseding clomiphene citrate based on evidence of superior live birth rates. It is commenced at 2.5 mg daily on cycle days 2-6 with ultrasound follicular tracking. This patient has completed appropriate lifestyle modification and has confirmed tubal patency, making pharmacological ovulation induction the logical next step.
key_points: Letrozole is first-line for ovulation induction in PCOS with superior live birth rates compared to clomiphene
Starting dose is 2.5 mg daily on cycle days 2-6 with dose escalation to 5 mg then 7.5 mg if no response
Ultrasound follicular tracking is recommended to monitor response and reduce multiple pregnancy risk
explanation.pearls: Letrozole achieves ovulation in approximately 60-80% of women with PCOS at the initial dose of 2.5 mg. If no dominant follicle develops, the dose can be increased to 5 mg in the next cycle and up to 7.5 mg. The cumulative live birth rate over 5 cycles of letrozole is approximately 28% compared to 19% with clomiphene. Women with PCOS and a high AMH above 40 pmol/L are at increased risk of ovarian hyperstimulation with gonadotrophins, further supporting the use of oral agents first.
